ml-vs-dl-pipeline.caption: In **classical ML**, a person must hand-design the "features" (the useful signals) before a model can use them — a slow, expertise-heavy bottleneck (shown in red). In **deep learning**, the network learns those features by itself, layer by layer, straight from the raw data.
ml-vs-dl-pipeline.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 660 380" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Comparison of classical machine learning and deep learning pipelines. Classical ML needs hand-crafted features designed by humans; deep learning learns features automatically.">
  <!-- ===================== TOP: Classical ML ===================== -->
  <text x="20" y="34" class="t-lg t-bold" text-anchor="start">Classical ML</text>

  <!-- Raw data -->
  <rect x="20" y="48" width="118" height="56" rx="6" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="79" y="80" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">Raw data</text>

  <!-- arrow -->
  <line x1="138" y1="76" x2="172" y2="76" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- Hand-crafted features (bottleneck) -->
  <rect x="172" y="48" width="138" height="56" rx="6" class="f-bad-soft s-bad" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text x="241" y="72" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">Hand-crafted</text>
  <text x="241" y="88" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">features</text>

  <!-- arrow -->
  <line x1="310" y1="76" x2="344" y2="76" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- ML model -->
  <rect x="344" y="48" width="118" height="56" rx="6" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="403" y="80" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">ML model</text>

  <!-- arrow -->
  <line x1="462" y1="76" x2="496" y2="76" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- Prediction -->
  <rect x="496" y="48" width="118" height="56" rx="6" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="555" y="80" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">Prediction</text>

  <!-- bottleneck label under hand-crafted features -->
  <line x1="241" y1="104" x2="241" y2="124" class="s-bad" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="241" y="139" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bad t-sm t-bold">bottleneck:</text>
  <text x="241" y="154" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bad t-sm">needs human expertise</text>

  <!-- divider -->
  <line x1="20" y1="190" x2="640" y2="190" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5 5"/>

  <!-- ===================== BOTTOM: Deep learning ===================== -->
  <text x="20" y="228" class="t-lg t-bold" text-anchor="start">Deep learning</text>

  <!-- Raw data -->
  <rect x="20" y="248" width="118" height="72" rx="6" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="79" y="288" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">Raw data</text>

  <!-- arrow -->
  <line x1="138" y1="284" x2="172" y2="284" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- Learned features wide box -->
  <rect x="172" y="248" width="290" height="72" rx="6" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text x="317" y="266" text-anchor="middle" class="t-accent t-sm t-bold">Learned features (auto)</text>

  <!-- three sub-blocks -->
  <rect x="190" y="276" width="76" height="32" rx="4" class="f-accent s-accent" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="228" y="296" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">layer 1</text>
  <line x1="266" y1="292" x2="283" y2="292" class="s-accent" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>

  <rect x="283" y="276" width="76" height="32" rx="4" class="f-accent s-accent" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="321" y="296" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">layer 2</text>
  <line x1="359" y1="292" x2="376" y2="292" class="s-accent" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>

  <rect x="376" y="276" width="76" height="32" rx="4" class="f-accent s-accent" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="414" y="296" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">layer 3</text>

  <!-- arrow -->
  <line x1="462" y1="284" x2="496" y2="284" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- Prediction -->
  <rect x="496" y="248" width="118" height="72" rx="6" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="555" y="288" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">Prediction</text>

  <!-- bottom note -->
  <text x="317" y="346" text-anchor="middle" class="t-accent t-sm">features learned automatically from data</text>
</svg>

representation-hierarchy.caption: A deep network builds understanding in **layers**: it starts from raw pixels, combines them into simple **edges and blobs**, then into **textures and parts** like eyes or wheels, and finally into whole **objects** such as a face or car. Reading bottom to top, each level is more abstract than the one below it.
representation-hierarchy.svg:

  <rect x="70" y="46" width="376" height="70" rx="6" class="f-accent s-line" stroke-width="1.8"/>
  <text x="86" y="86" class="t-bold">Objects (face, car)</text>
  <circle cx="410" cy="81" r="19" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.8"/>
  <circle cx="403" cy="76" r="2" class="f-muted"/>

  <ellipse cx="392" cy="161" rx="13" ry="7" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <circle cx="392" cy="161" r="3.5" class="f-muted"/>
  <circle cx="426" cy="161" r="10" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <circle cx="426" cy="161" r="2.5" class="f-muted"/>
  <line x1="426" y1="151" x2="426" y2="171" class="s-line" stroke-width="1"/>

  <ellipse cx="426" cy="249" rx="9" ry="6" class="f-panel2 s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>

  <circle cx="392" cy="308" r="2.6" class="f-muted s-line" stroke-width="1"/>
  <circle cx="404" cy="308" r="2.6" class="f-muted s-line" stroke-width="1"/>

</svg>

universal-approximation.caption: A neural network builds up a complicated curve by stacking many simple **bumps**: the green stair-step (the network's output) traces the smooth orange **target** more and more closely as you add more bumps. With enough of them, a network can approximate essentially *any* curve.
universal-approximation.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 560 360" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Universal approximation: a smooth target curve approximated by summing several simple bump functions into a stair-step network output.">
  <!-- axes -->
  <line x1="60" y1="40" x2="60" y2="290" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="60" y1="290" x2="520" y2="290" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <text x="40" y="46" class="t-sm t-muted" text-anchor="middle">y</text>
  <text x="528" y="306" class="t-sm t-muted" text-anchor="start">x</text>

  <!-- bumps (pale, muted) -->
  <path d="M100 290 C120 290 122 200 142 200 C162 200 164 290 184 290" class="f-muted s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <path d="M184 290 C204 290 206 150 226 150 C246 150 248 290 268 290" class="f-muted s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <path d="M268 290 C288 290 290 175 310 175 C330 175 332 290 352 290" class="f-muted s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <path d="M352 290 C372 290 374 215 394 215 C414 215 416 290 436 290" class="f-muted s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>

  <!-- network output: stair-step approximation (ok) -->
  <polyline points="100,255 142,255 142,180 226,180 226,140 310,140 310,170 394,170 394,225 436,225" class="f-none s-ok" stroke-width="2.5"/>

  <!-- target curve (accent) -->
  <path d="M100 235 C160 110 220 110 268 150 C320 195 360 130 436 235" class="f-none s-accent" stroke-width="2.5"/>

  <!-- legend -->
  <rect x="300" y="48" width="218" height="64" rx="8" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="314" y1="70" x2="346" y2="70" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text x="354" y="74" class="t-sm" text-anchor="start">target function</text>
  <polyline points="314,94 322,94 322,86 338,86 338,94 346,94" class="f-none s-ok" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text x="354" y="98" class="t-sm" text-anchor="start">network output (sum of bumps)</text>

  <!-- caption note -->
  <text x="100" y="322" class="t-sm t-muted" text-anchor="start">each simple bump adds one step</text>
  <text x="100" y="340" class="t-sm t-muted" text-anchor="start">more bumps → closer fit to any curve</text>
</svg>

perceptron.caption: A **perceptron** multiplies each input ($x_1, x_2, x_3$) by its own **weight**, adds them up along with a **bias** $b$, and then passes that total through an **activation function** $f$ to produce the output $y$.
perceptron.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 580 360" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="A single perceptron: three weighted inputs plus a bias are summed, passed through an activation function f, and produce output y.">
  <!-- Input nodes -->
  <circle cx="70" cy="90" r="26" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="70" y="95" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">x1</text>
  <circle cx="70" cy="180" r="26" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="70" y="185" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">x2</text>
  <circle cx="70" cy="270" r="26" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="70" y="275" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">x3</text>

  <!-- Weighted connections to sum node -->
  <line x1="94" y1="100" x2="240" y2="163" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <line x1="96" y1="180" x2="236" y2="180" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <line x1="94" y1="260" x2="240" y2="197" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>

  <!-- Weight labels -->
  <text x="165" y="123" text-anchor="middle" class="t-accent t-sm t-bold">w1</text>
  <text x="166" y="171" text-anchor="middle" class="t-accent t-sm t-bold">w2</text>
  <text x="165" y="240" text-anchor="middle" class="t-accent t-sm t-bold">w3</text>

  <!-- Bias arrow from above -->
  <line x1="280" y1="84" x2="280" y2="138" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <text x="280" y="72" text-anchor="middle" class="t-accent t-sm t-bold">+ b (bias)</text>

  <!-- Sum node -->
  <circle cx="280" cy="180" r="38" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text x="280" y="190" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-accent">Σ</text>

  <!-- Sum -> activation -->
  <line x1="324" y1="180" x2="364" y2="180" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- Activation box -->
  <rect x="368" y="150" width="110" height="60" rx="14" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="423" y="180" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg">f</text>
  <text x="423" y="200" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">activation</text>

  <!-- activation -> output -->
  <line x1="478" y1="180" x2="500" y2="180" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- Output node -->
  <circle cx="528" cy="180" r="22" class="f-ok-soft s-ok" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="528" y="185" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold t-ok">y</text>

  <!-- Formulas -->
  <text x="290" y="312" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">z = w1·x1 + w2·x2 + w3·x3 + b</text>
  <text x="290" y="336" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">output = f(z)</text>
</svg>

activation-functions.caption: **Activation functions** decide how strongly a neuron "fires" for a given input. **Sigmoid** squashes values into $0$ to $1$ and **tanh** into $-1$ to $1$, while **ReLU** simply passes positive values and zeroes out negatives; **Leaky ReLU** lets a small slope through on the negative side so those neurons never go fully dead.
activation-functions.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 540 420" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Four activation function mini-plots: sigmoid (0 to 1 S-curve), tanh (-1 to 1 S-curve), ReLU (flat then 45-degree line), and leaky ReLU (gentle negative slope then steeper positive slope).">
  <!-- ===== Panels ===== -->
  <rect x="16" y="16" width="246" height="186" rx="8" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="278" y="16" width="246" height="186" rx="8" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="16" y="218" width="246" height="186" rx="8" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="278" y="218" width="246" height="186" rx="8" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>

  <!-- ===== Cell 1: Sigmoid ===== -->
  <text x="139" y="36" class="t-bold t-sm" text-anchor="middle">Sigmoid</text>
  <line x1="50" y1="46" x2="50" y2="174" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="50" y1="174" x2="238" y2="174" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="144" y1="46" x2="144" y2="174" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="44" y="50" class="t-sm t-muted" text-anchor="end">1</text>

  <polyline points="50,173 69,172 88,168 106,159 125,140 144,110 163,80 182,61 200,52 219,48 238,47" class="s-accent" fill="none" stroke-width="2.5"/>

  <!-- ===== Cell 2: Tanh ===== -->
  <text x="401" y="36" class="t-bold t-sm" text-anchor="middle">Tanh</text>
  <line x1="312" y1="46" x2="312" y2="174" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="312" y1="110" x2="500" y2="110" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="406" y1="46" x2="406" y2="174" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="306" y="50" class="t-sm t-muted" text-anchor="end">1</text>
  <text x="306" y="114" class="t-sm t-muted" text-anchor="end">0</text>
  <text x="306" y="178" class="t-sm t-muted" text-anchor="end">-1</text>
  <polyline points="312,174 340,173 368,171 387,159 397,140 406,110 415,80 425,61 444,49 472,47 500,46" class="s-accent" fill="none" stroke-width="2.5"/>

  <!-- ===== Cell 3: ReLU ===== -->
  <text x="139" y="238" class="t-bold t-sm" text-anchor="middle">ReLU</text>
  <line x1="50" y1="248" x2="50" y2="376" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="50" y1="376" x2="238" y2="376" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="144" y1="248" x2="144" y2="376" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="44" y="380" class="t-sm t-muted" text-anchor="end">0</text>
  <polyline points="50,376 144,376 238,282" class="s-accent" fill="none" stroke-width="2.5"/>

  <!-- ===== Cell 4: Leaky ReLU ===== -->
  <text x="401" y="238" class="t-bold t-sm" text-anchor="middle">Leaky ReLU</text>
  <line x1="312" y1="248" x2="312" y2="376" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="312" y1="344" x2="500" y2="344" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="406" y1="248" x2="406" y2="376" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="306" y="348" class="t-sm t-muted" text-anchor="end">0</text>
  <polyline points="312,363 406,344 500,256" class="s-accent" fill="none" stroke-width="2.5"/>
</svg>

mlp-architecture.caption: A **multi-layer perceptron**: information flows left to right from 3 input nodes through two hidden layers of 5 nodes each to 2 output nodes. Every node connects to all nodes in the next layer, which is why it is called **fully connected**.
mlp-architecture.svg:

xor-problem.caption: The four XOR cases sit at the corners of a square: matching inputs $(0,0)$ and $(1,1)$ are **class 0** (green circles), while differing inputs $(0,1)$ and $(1,0)$ are **class 1** (red squares). No matter how you draw a single straight line, each side ends up mixing both classes, so a single perceptron cannot solve XOR.
xor-problem.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 480 360" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="A 2D plane with the four XOR points. Inputs (0,0) and (1,1) are class 0, inputs (0,1) and (1,0) are class 1. A dashed line cannot separate the two classes, so no single straight line works.">
  <text x="240" y="26" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg">XOR is not linearly separable</text>

  <line x1="120" y1="270" x2="374" y2="270" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="140" y1="292" x2="140" y2="64" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <line x1="340" y1="266" x2="340" y2="274" class="s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="136" y1="90" x2="144" y2="90" class="s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>

  <text x="366" y="288" text-anchor="middle" class="t-muted">x1</text>
  <text x="126" y="58" text-anchor="middle" class="t-muted">x2</text>
  <text x="126" y="290" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">0</text>
  <text x="340" y="290" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">1</text>
  <text x="122" y="94" text-anchor="end" class="t-sm t-muted">1</text>

  <line x1="210" y1="66" x2="270" y2="282" class="s-muted" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="6 5"/>
  <text x="236" y="52" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-bold t-bad">no single line works</text>

  <circle cx="140" cy="270" r="13" class="f-ok-soft s-ok" stroke-width="2"/>
  <circle cx="340" cy="90" r="13" class="f-ok-soft s-ok" stroke-width="2"/>
  <rect x="127" y="77" width="26" height="26" class="f-bad-soft s-bad" stroke-width="2"/>
  <rect x="327" y="257" width="26" height="26" class="f-bad-soft s-bad" stroke-width="2"/>

  <circle cx="150" cy="320" r="9" class="f-ok-soft s-ok" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="165" y="324" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm">class 0  (XOR = 0)</text>
  <rect x="291" y="311" width="18" height="18" class="f-bad-soft s-bad" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="315" y="324" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm">class 1  (XOR = 1)</text>
</svg>

computational-graph.caption: A **computational graph** breaks a formula into tiny steps: here $x{+}y$ gives $q=5$, then $q{\times}z$ gives $f=20$. Black arrows show the **forward pass** (plugging in numbers), while the blue labels show the **backward pass** — how much $f$ changes if each input nudges, which is exactly what backpropagation computes.
computational-graph.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 620 322" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Computational graph: inputs x, y, z feed a plus node producing q, then a times node producing f, with forward values and backward gradient labels.">
  <!-- forward edges -->
  <line x1="99" y1="75" x2="247" y2="105" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="99" y1="145" x2="247" y2="116" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="301" y1="119" x2="434" y2="166" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="99" y1="227" x2="432" y2="179" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="488" y1="175" x2="517" y2="175" class="s-ok" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-ok)"/>

  <!-- input nodes -->
  <circle cx="75" cy="70" r="24" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <circle cx="75" cy="150" r="24" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <circle cx="75" cy="230" r="24" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="75" y="76" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg">x</text>
  <text x="75" y="156" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg">y</text>
  <text x="75" y="236" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg">z</text>
  <text x="75" y="110" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">x = 2</text>
  <text x="75" y="190" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">y = 3</text>
  <text x="75" y="270" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">z = 4</text>

  <!-- operation nodes -->
  <circle cx="275" cy="110" r="28" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2"/>
  <circle cx="460" cy="175" r="28" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="275" y="119" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-accent">+</text>
  <text x="460" y="183" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-accent">×</text>

  <!-- result -->
  <rect x="520" y="159" width="84" height="32" rx="8" class="f-ok-soft s-ok" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="562" y="180" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold t-ok">f = 20</text>

  <!-- forward value on q edge -->
  <text x="335" y="116" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-bold">q = 5</text>

  <!-- backward gradient labels -->
  <text x="173" y="104" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent">dq/dx = 1</text>
  <text x="173" y="143" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent">dq/dy = 1</text>
  <text x="372" y="161" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent">df/dq = z = 4</text>
  <text x="250" y="216" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent">df/dz = q = 5</text>

  <!-- legend -->
  <line x1="80" y1="300" x2="106" y2="300" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <text x="112" y="304" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm t-muted">forward: compute values</text>
  <text x="320" y="304" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm t-accent">backward: gradients (df/d·)</text>
</svg>

forward-backward.caption: The **forward pass** (green, left to right) pushes input data through the layers to produce a prediction and its **Loss**. The **backward pass** (red, right to left) then sends that error signal back through the network as gradients, so each weight learns how to improve.
forward-backward.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 600 360" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="A three-layer neural network: a green forward pass flows left to right to compute a prediction, and a red backward pass flows right to left from the Loss box to propagate gradients.">
  <!-- connections: input -> hidden -->
  <line x1="120" y1="120" x2="255" y2="105" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="120" y1="120" x2="255" y2="155" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1"/>

  <line x1="120" y1="175" x2="255" y2="205" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="120" y1="175" x2="255" y2="255" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="120" y1="230" x2="255" y2="105" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="120" y1="230" x2="255" y2="155" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="120" y1="230" x2="255" y2="205" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="120" y1="230" x2="255" y2="255" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1"/>
  <!-- connections: hidden -> output -->

  <line x1="255" y1="105" x2="385" y2="205" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="255" y1="155" x2="385" y2="155" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="255" y1="155" x2="385" y2="205" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="255" y1="205" x2="385" y2="155" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1"/>

chain-rule.caption: The **chain rule** says that to find how the final result $L$ changes with the input $x$, you just **multiply the small slopes step by step** along the path: $dL/dx = dL/du \times du/dx$.
chain-rule.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 560 280" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Chain rule diagram: x leads to u leads to L, with local slopes du/dx and dL/du multiplied to give dL/dx.">
  <!-- tiny role labels above boxes -->
  <text x="95" y="44" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">input</text>
  <text x="280" y="44" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">intermediate</text>
  <text x="465" y="44" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">loss</text>

  <!-- boxes -->
  <rect x="60" y="56" width="70" height="52" rx="8" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <rect x="245" y="56" width="70" height="52" rx="8" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <rect x="430" y="56" width="70" height="52" rx="8" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>

  <!-- box letters -->
  <text x="95" y="91" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-bold">x</text>
  <text x="280" y="91" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-bold">u</text>
  <text x="465" y="91" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-bold">L</text>

  <!-- forward arrows -->
  <line x1="134" y1="82" x2="241" y2="82" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="319" y1="82" x2="426" y2="82" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- local slope labels under arrows -->
  <text x="188" y="126" text-anchor="middle" class="t-accent">du/dx</text>
  <text x="373" y="126" text-anchor="middle" class="t-accent">dL/du</text>

  <!-- big accent equation -->
  <text x="280" y="192" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-accent">dL/dx = dL/du × du/dx</text>

  <!-- tiny note -->
  <text x="280" y="230" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">multiply the local slopes along the path</text>
</svg>

loss-landscape.caption: The rings are **contour lines** of the loss, like a map of a valley — the centre star is the lowest point. Starting from the outside, each arrow is one **gradient-descent step** that moves a little downhill, so repeating the steps walks you down to the **minimum**.
loss-landscape.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 560 400" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Gradient descent stepping down a tilted elliptical loss valley toward the minimum">
  <text x="280" y="28" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg">Gradient descent on a loss surface</text>

  <!-- Loss contours: concentric tilted ellipses (a valley) -->
  <ellipse cx="300" cy="220" rx="175" ry="105" transform="rotate(-25 300 220)" class="f-none s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <ellipse cx="300" cy="220" rx="130" ry="78" transform="rotate(-25 300 220)" class="f-none s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <ellipse cx="300" cy="220" rx="85" ry="51" transform="rotate(-25 300 220)" class="f-none s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <ellipse cx="300" cy="220" rx="40" ry="24" transform="rotate(-25 300 220)" class="f-accent-soft s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>

  <text x="432" y="112" text-anchor="start" class="t-muted t-sm">loss contours</text>

  <!-- Gradient-descent steps: arrows stepping inward -->
  <line x1="140" y1="110" x2="183" y2="144" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <line x1="188" y1="148" x2="223" y2="172" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <line x1="228" y1="175" x2="257" y2="194" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <line x1="262" y1="197" x2="283" y2="209" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <line x1="288" y1="212" x2="296" y2="217" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>

  <!-- Start point -->
  <circle cx="140" cy="110" r="4" class="f-accent s-accent" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="140" y="98" text-anchor="middle" class="t-accent t-sm t-bold">start</text>

  <!-- Minimum (star) -->
  <polygon points="300,211 302,217 309,217 303,221 305,227 300,224 295,227 297,221 291,217 298,217" class="f-accent s-accent" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="316" y="224" text-anchor="start" class="t-accent t-sm t-bold">minimum</text>

  <!-- Legend -->
  <line x1="24" y1="374" x2="54" y2="374" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <text x="62" y="378" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm">each step moves downhill (-gradient)</text>
</svg>

gradient-descent-1d.caption: Gradient descent nudges a parameter **downhill** on the loss curve: it measures the **slope** under the ball, then takes a step in the opposite direction sized by the **learning rate**, edging closer to the lowest-loss point each time.
gradient-descent-1d.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 520 360" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="One step of gradient descent rolling a ball downhill on a 1D loss curve toward its minimum.">
  <!-- axes -->
  <line x1="70" y1="300" x2="70" y2="38" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="70" y1="300" x2="492" y2="300" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <text x="34" y="170" transform="rotate(-90 34 170)" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">loss</text>
  <text x="282" y="332" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">parameter</text>

  <!-- U-shaped loss curve -->
  <path d="M95 99 Q280 441 465 99" class="f-none s-line" stroke-width="2.5"/>

  <!-- tangent line (slope at the ball) -->
  <line x1="143" y1="180" x2="203" y2="246" class="s-muted" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="200" y="266" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">slope</text>

  <!-- downhill step arrow -->
  <line x1="188" y1="200" x2="276" y2="200" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <text x="236" y="184" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent t-bold">step = -learning_rate × slope</text>

  <!-- ball on the curve -->
  <circle cx="170" cy="210" r="9" class="f-accent s-accent" stroke-width="2"/>

  <!-- minimum marker -->
  <polygon points="280,264 286,270 280,276 274,270" class="f-ok s-ok" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="280" y="292" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-ok t-bold">minimum</text>
</svg>

learning-rate-effects.caption: The **learning rate** sets how big each step is when a model rolls "downhill" to reduce its error. Too small and it crawls and barely improves; **just right** and it settles neatly at the bottom; too large and it overshoots back and forth, getting worse instead of better.
learning-rate-effects.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 660 290" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Three loss curves comparing learning rates: too small crawls slowly, just right reaches the minimum, too large overshoots and diverges.">
  <text x="330" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-bold">Effect of the learning rate</text>

  <!-- ===== Panel 1: Too small ===== -->
  <rect x="20" y="48" width="190" height="212" rx="10" class="f-panel s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="115" y="68" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold t-muted">Too small</text>
  <text x="115" y="84" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">slow crawl</text>
  <!-- axes -->

  <!-- loss curve -->

  <!-- target minimum (not reached) -->

  <!-- tiny steps -->

  <line x1="67" y1="155" x2="73" y2="168" class="s-muted" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="73" y1="168" x2="79" y2="178" class="s-muted" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <circle cx="62" cy="142" r="5" class="f-muted s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>

  <!-- ===== Panel 2: Just right ===== -->

  <text x="325" y="68" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold t-ok">Just right</text>
  <text x="325" y="84" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">reaches bottom</text>
  <!-- axes -->
  <line x1="250" y1="225" x2="250" y2="80" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="250" y1="225" x2="402" y2="225" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <!-- loss curve -->

  <!-- decisive steps -->

  <line x1="290" y1="181" x2="311" y2="205" class="s-ok" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-ok)"/>
  <line x1="311" y1="205" x2="322" y2="210" class="s-ok" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-ok)"/>
  <circle cx="269" cy="135" r="5" class="f-ok-soft s-ok" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <circle cx="325" cy="210" r="6" class="f-ok s-ok" stroke-width="1.5"/>

  <line x1="460" y1="225" x2="612" y2="225" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <!-- loss curve -->

  <!-- target minimum (overshot) -->

  <!-- diverging zigzag -->

  <line x1="591" y1="135" x2="601" y2="112" class="s-bad" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-bad)"/>

</svg>

optimizer-paths.caption: Each curve traces how a different optimizer travels downhill across the **loss landscape** toward the lowest point. **SGD** stumbles in jagged zig-zags, **Momentum** swings more smoothly, and **Adam** takes the fastest, most direct route to the minimum.
optimizer-paths.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 560 380" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Loss landscape contours with three optimizer descent paths: SGD zig-zags, Momentum oscillates more smoothly, and Adam takes a fast direct route to the minimum.">
  <!-- Elliptical loss contours -->
  <ellipse cx="330" cy="200" rx="200" ry="128" class="f-none s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <ellipse cx="330" cy="200" rx="156" ry="100" class="f-none s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <ellipse cx="330" cy="200" rx="112" ry="72" class="f-none s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <ellipse cx="330" cy="200" rx="70" ry="45" class="f-none s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <ellipse cx="330" cy="200" rx="32" ry="20" class="f-none s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>

  <!-- Minimum dot -->
  <circle cx="330" cy="200" r="6" class="f-accent s-accent" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="330" y="184" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">minimum</text>

  <!-- Start point -->
  <circle cx="118" cy="92" r="6" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="118" y="78" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-bold">start</text>

  <!-- SGD: jagged zig-zag (muted) -->
  <polyline points="118,92 168,150 150,178 210,196 188,232 256,228 240,252 296,234 318,212 330,200"
            class="f-none s-muted" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- Momentum: smoother oscillation (accent2) -->
  <path d="M118,92 C150,128 188,108 220,150 C252,192 268,168 300,196 C316,210 322,202 330,200"
        class="f-none s-accent2" stroke-width="2.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>

  <!-- Adam: fast fairly-direct path (accent) -->
  <path d="M118,92 C176,118 232,150 282,176 C306,188 320,196 330,200"
        class="f-none s-accent" stroke-width="2.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>

  <!-- Legend -->
  <rect x="22" y="290" width="170" height="78" rx="8" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="36" y1="310" x2="68" y2="310" class="s-muted" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="78" y="314" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm">SGD  (zig-zag)</text>
  <line x1="36" y1="332" x2="68" y2="332" class="s-accent2" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text x="78" y="336" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm">Momentum</text>
  <line x1="36" y1="354" x2="68" y2="354" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text x="78" y="358" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm">Adam  (direct)</text>
</svg>

cross-entropy-loss.caption: Cross-entropy loss is $-\log(p)$, where $p$ is the probability the model assigned to the **correct** answer. When the model is confident and right ($p\to1$) the loss is tiny, but being confident and **wrong** ($p\to0$) sends the loss soaring — so the curve heavily punishes confident mistakes.
cross-entropy-loss.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 480 320" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Cross-entropy loss curve: loss equals minus log of the predicted probability of the true class. Loss is huge when the model is confidently wrong (p near 0) and near zero when confidently correct (p near 1).">
  <!-- y-axis -->
  <line x1="70" y1="250" x2="70" y2="44" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <!-- x-axis -->
  <line x1="70" y1="250" x2="452" y2="250" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- y ticks -->
  <line x1="66" y1="207" x2="70" y2="207" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="66" y1="163" x2="70" y2="163" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="66" y1="120" x2="70" y2="120" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="66" y1="76" x2="70" y2="76" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="60" y="211" text-anchor="end" class="t-muted t-sm">1</text>
  <text x="60" y="167" text-anchor="end" class="t-muted t-sm">2</text>
  <text x="60" y="124" text-anchor="end" class="t-muted t-sm">3</text>
  <text x="60" y="80" text-anchor="end" class="t-muted t-sm">4</text>
  <text x="60" y="254" text-anchor="end" class="t-muted t-sm">0</text>

  <!-- x ticks -->
  <line x1="255" y1="250" x2="255" y2="254" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="70" y="267" text-anchor="middle" class="t-muted t-sm">0</text>
  <text x="255" y="267" text-anchor="middle" class="t-muted t-sm">0.5</text>
  <text x="440" y="267" text-anchor="middle" class="t-muted t-sm">1</text>

  <!-- axis labels -->
  <text x="255" y="290" text-anchor="middle" class="t-muted">predicted probability of TRUE class (p)</text>
  <text x="28" y="150" text-anchor="middle" class="t-muted" transform="rotate(-90 28 150)">loss</text>

  <!-- loss = -log(p) curve -->
  <polyline points="74,50 77,80 89,120 107,150 126,168 144,180 181,198 218,210 255,220 292,228 329,234 366,240 403,245 440,250" fill="none" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2.5"/>

  <!-- formula -->

  <!-- anchor dots -->
  <circle cx="89" cy="120" r="4" class="f-bad s-bad" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <circle cx="403" cy="245" r="4" class="f-ok s-ok" stroke-width="1.5"/>

  <!-- wrong annotation -->
  <line x1="150" y1="92" x2="95" y2="119" class="s-bad" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-bad)"/>
  <text x="205" y="68" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bad t-bold">confident & wrong</text>
  <text x="205" y="84" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bad">huge loss</text>

  <!-- correct annotation -->
  <line x1="388" y1="202" x2="414" y2="240" class="s-ok" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-ok)"/>
  <text x="350" y="176" text-anchor="middle" class="t-ok t-bold">confident & correct</text>
  <text x="350" y="192" text-anchor="middle" class="t-ok">low loss</text>
</svg>

lr-schedules.caption: Three common ways to shrink the **learning rate** as training proceeds: **step decay** drops it in sudden jumps, **exponential** decays it smoothly, and **cosine annealing** eases it gently down to near $0$. All start high so the model learns fast early, then slow down to fine-tune.
lr-schedules.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 520 350" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Plot comparing three learning-rate schedules over epochs: step decay as a descending staircase, exponential as a smooth decay, and cosine annealing curving down to near zero.">
  <!-- y-axis -->
  <line class="s-line" x1="70" y1="290" x2="70" y2="40" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <!-- x-axis -->
  <line class="s-line" x1="70" y1="290" x2="492" y2="290" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- y tick labels -->
  <text class="t-sm t-muted" x="62" y="54" text-anchor="end">high</text>
  <text class="t-sm t-muted" x="62" y="294" text-anchor="end">0</text>

  <!-- axis titles -->
  <text class="t-sm" x="281" y="322" text-anchor="middle">epoch</text>
  <text class="t-sm" x="28" y="165" text-anchor="middle" transform="rotate(-90 28 165)">learning rate</text>

  <!-- Step decay (s-accent) staircase -->
  <polyline class="s-accent f-none" fill="none" stroke-width="2.5"
    points="70,50 173,50 173,170 275,170 275,230 378,230 378,260 480,260"/>

  <!-- Exponential (s-ok) smooth decay -->
  <polyline class="s-ok f-none" fill="none" stroke-width="2.5"
    points="70,50 111,112 152,158 193,192 234,218 275,237 316,250 357,261 398,268 439,274 480,278"/>

  <!-- Cosine annealing (s-accent2) -->
  <polyline class="s-accent2 f-none" fill="none" stroke-width="2.5"
    points="70,50 111,56 152,73 193,100 234,133 275,170 316,207 357,240 398,267 439,284 480,290"/>

  <!-- legend -->
  <rect class="f-panel s-line" x="343" y="46" width="137" height="74" rx="6" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line class="s-accent" x1="352" y1="66" x2="374" y2="66" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text class="t-sm" x="380" y="70" text-anchor="start">Step decay</text>
  <line class="s-ok" x1="352" y1="86" x2="374" y2="86" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text class="t-sm" x="380" y="90" text-anchor="start">Exponential</text>
  <line class="s-accent2" x1="352" y1="106" x2="374" y2="106" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text class="t-sm" x="380" y="110" text-anchor="start">Cosine annealing</text>
</svg>

overfitting-curve.caption: As training continues, the model's error on the **training data** keeps dropping, but its error on **new (validation) data** stops at a low point and then climbs as the model starts memorizing noise. **Early stopping** halts training at that sweet spot — the boundary between underfitting and overfitting.
overfitting-curve.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 560 380" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Training error keeps falling while validation error falls then rises; early stopping marks the validation minimum between underfitting and overfitting regions.">
  <!-- region shading -->
  <rect x="70" y="40" width="170" height="250" class="f-accent-soft s-none" />
  <rect x="240" y="40" width="220" height="250" class="f-bad-soft s-none" />

  <!-- region labels -->
  <text x="155" y="62" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">underfitting</text>
  <text x="350" y="62" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-bad">overfitting</text>

  <!-- axes -->
  <line x1="70" y1="290" x2="470" y2="290" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)" />
  <line x1="70" y1="290" x2="70" y2="40" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)" />

  <!-- axis labels -->
  <text x="270" y="322" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">training epochs →</text>
  <text x="42" y="165" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm" transform="rotate(-90 42 165)">error →</text>

  <!-- training error: steadily down toward 0 -->
  <path d="M 80 95 C 160 130, 200 235, 280 262 S 400 282, 460 285" class="s-ok" stroke-width="2.5" fill="none" />

  <!-- validation error: U-shape -->
  <path d="M 80 120 C 150 165, 210 218, 240 220 C 320 226, 360 200, 460 130" class="s-bad" stroke-width="2.5" fill="none" />

  <!-- early stop vertical line at validation minimum -->
  <line x1="240" y1="40" x2="240" y2="290" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="6 5" />
  <circle cx="240" cy="220" r="4.5" class="f-accent s-accent" stroke-width="1.5" />
  <text x="240" y="32" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent t-bold">early stop</text>

  <!-- legend -->
  <rect x="312" y="332" width="206" height="34" rx="6" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5" />
  <line x1="324" y1="349" x2="352" y2="349" class="s-ok" stroke-width="2.5" />
  <text x="358" y="353" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm">training</text>
  <line x1="418" y1="349" x2="446" y2="349" class="s-bad" stroke-width="2.5" />
  <text x="452" y="353" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm">validation</text>
</svg>

dropout.caption: During training, **dropout** randomly switches off some neurons (the greyed-out ones marked $\times$) so their connections vanish for that step. Because the network can never rely on any single neuron, it learns **redundant, more robust** features instead of overfitting.

l1-vs-l2.caption: Both panels show the loss landscape (the rings) plus a "budget" that forces the weights to stay small (the colored shape); the best allowed model sits where the smallest ring touches that shape. **L2** uses a round budget, so the solution usually keeps two small but nonzero weights, while **L1**'s pointed diamond has corners on the axes, so the solution often lands exactly on an axis — pushing a weight to **zero** and giving a sparse model.
l1-vs-l2.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 640 330" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Two weight-space panels comparing L2 circular constraint versus L1 diamond constraint, showing how L1 drives a weight to zero">
  <!-- ===== panels ===== -->
  <rect class="f-panel s-line" x="16" y="44" width="300" height="268" rx="8" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect class="f-panel s-line" x="324" y="44" width="300" height="268" rx="8" stroke-width="1.5"/>

  <!-- titles -->
  <text class="t-bold" x="166" y="66" text-anchor="middle">L2  (weight decay)</text>
  <text class="t-bold" x="474" y="66" text-anchor="middle">L1  (lasso)</text>
  <text class="t-sm t-muted" x="166" y="82" text-anchor="middle">ellipses = loss contours</text>
  <text class="t-sm t-muted" x="474" y="82" text-anchor="middle">ellipses = loss contours</text>

  <!-- ============ LEFT : L2 ============ -->
  <!-- axes -->
  <line class="s-line" x1="40" y1="242" x2="300" y2="242" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line class="s-line" x1="115" y1="296" x2="115" y2="90" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- L2 constraint ball -->

  <text class="t-sm t-accent" x="70" y="282" text-anchor="end">L2 ball</text>

  <!-- loss contours -->
  <ellipse class="f-none s-muted" cx="185" cy="160" rx="78" ry="56" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <ellipse class="f-none s-muted" cx="185" cy="160" rx="54" ry="39" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <ellipse class="f-none s-muted" cx="185" cy="160" rx="30" ry="22" stroke-width="1.5"/>

  <text class="t-sm t-muted" x="198" y="156" text-anchor="start">min</text>

  <!-- solution (off both axes) -->

  <text class="t-sm t-accent2" x="92" y="178" text-anchor="end">solution</text>

  <text class="t-sm" x="166" y="304" text-anchor="middle">solution off the axes: weights small but nonzero</text>

  <!-- ============ RIGHT : L1 ============ -->

  <text class="t-sm t-muted" x="437" y="98" text-anchor="start">w2</text>
  <text class="t-sm t-muted" x="422" y="256" text-anchor="end">0</text>

  <!-- L1 constraint ball (diamond) -->
  <polygon class="f-accent-soft s-accent" points="476,242 430,196 384,242 430,288" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text class="t-sm t-accent" x="392" y="280" text-anchor="end">L1 ball</text>

  <!-- loss contours -->
  <ellipse class="f-none s-muted" cx="450" cy="150" rx="44" ry="52" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <ellipse class="f-none s-muted" cx="450" cy="150" rx="30" ry="36" stroke-width="1.5"/>

  <!-- solution ON the w2 axis -> w1 = 0 -->

  <text class="t-sm t-bold t-accent2" x="440" y="192" text-anchor="start">w1 = 0</text>

  <text class="t-sm" x="474" y="304" text-anchor="middle">corner on axis: a weight becomes exactly 0 (sparse)</text>
</svg>

data-augmentation.caption: **Data augmentation** turns one labelled image into many by applying small changes — flipping, rotating, cropping (zooming), and adjusting brightness. The picture still shows the same thing, so the model sees more varied examples and **learns to generalize** without needing extra hand-labelled data.
data-augmentation.svg:

bias-variance.caption: As a model gets more complex, its **bias** (oversimplifying) shrinks while its **variance** (overreacting to the training data) grows. The **total error** is the sum of the two, so it dips to a lowest point — the **sweet spot** — where the model is neither too simple nor too complex.
bias-variance.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 560 360" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Bias-variance tradeoff: bias squared falls and variance rises as model complexity grows; their sum, the total error, is U-shaped and is lowest at the sweet spot.">
  <!-- legend (top) -->
  <line x1="80" y1="24" x2="102" y2="24" class="s-ok" stroke-width="3"/>
  <text x="108" y="28" class="t-sm">bias^2</text>
  <line x1="205" y1="24" x2="227" y2="24" class="s-bad" stroke-width="3"/>
  <text x="233" y="28" class="t-sm">variance</text>
  <line x1="330" y1="24" x2="352" y2="24" class="s-accent" stroke-width="3"/>
  <text x="358" y="28" class="t-sm">total error</text>

  <!-- axes -->
  <line x1="70" y1="300" x2="70" y2="46" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="70" y1="300" x2="530" y2="300" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- sweet-spot vertical line -->
  <line x1="295" y1="300" x2="295" y2="74" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="5 5"/>
  <text x="295" y="66" text-anchor="middle" class="t-accent t-bold t-sm">sweet spot</text>

  <!-- bias^2 (decreasing) -->
  <polyline points="70,84 115,143 160,186 205,217 250,240 295,256 340,268 385,277 430,283 475,288 520,291" class="f-none s-ok" stroke-width="2.5"/>

  <!-- variance (increasing) -->
  <polyline points="70,288 115,284 160,279 205,272 250,263 295,251 340,236 385,215 430,187 475,151 520,103" class="f-none s-bad" stroke-width="2.5"/>

  <!-- total error (U-shape = sum) -->
  <polyline points="70,72 115,127 160,165 205,189 250,203 295,208 340,204 385,192 430,171 475,139 520,94" class="f-none s-accent" stroke-width="2.5"/>

  <!-- minimum marker -->
  <circle cx="295" cy="208" r="4" class="f-accent s-accent" stroke-width="2"/>

  <!-- axis labels -->
  <text x="150" y="318" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">underfit</text>
  <text x="460" y="318" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">overfit</text>
  <text x="300" y="338" text-anchor="middle">model complexity</text>
  <text x="26" y="173" text-anchor="middle" transform="rotate(-90 26 173)">error</text>
</svg>

vanishing-exploding.caption: As an error signal travels backward through many layers, it gets multiplied again and again: factors a little **less than 1** make it fade to nearly nothing (**vanishing**), factors a little **more than 1** make it balloon (**exploding**), and only well-scaled weights keep it **stable**.
vanishing-exploding.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 560 380" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Plot of gradient magnitude on a log axis versus layer depth, showing a vanishing curve shrinking toward zero, a stable curve staying flat, and an exploding curve growing huge as gradients propagate back toward the input.">
  <!-- axes -->
  <line x1="72" y1="295" x2="72" y2="45" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="72" y1="295" x2="520" y2="295" class="s-line" stroke-width="2"/>

  <!-- reference: gradient = 1 -->
  <line x1="72" y1="175" x2="510" y2="175" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5 5"/>
  <text x="64" y="179" text-anchor="end" class="t-muted t-sm">≈1</text>

  <!-- curves (right = output, left = input) -->
  <path d="M 505 175 C 420 205, 230 275, 76 288" class="s-muted" fill="none" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <path d="M 505 175 C 390 172, 210 170, 76 168" class="s-ok" fill="none" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <path d="M 505 175 C 420 145, 240 80, 76 62" class="s-bad" fill="none" stroke-width="2.5"/>

  <!-- legend -->
  <rect x="368" y="56" width="142" height="66" rx="6" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="380" y1="74" x2="400" y2="74" class="s-muted" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text x="406" y="78" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm">vanishing</text>
  <line x1="380" y1="92" x2="400" y2="92" class="s-ok" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text x="406" y="96" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm">stable / well-scaled</text>
  <line x1="380" y1="110" x2="400" y2="110" class="s-bad" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text x="406" y="114" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm">exploding</text>

  <!-- axis endpoint labels -->
  <text x="80" y="312" text-anchor="start" class="t-muted t-sm">input</text>
  <text x="508" y="312" text-anchor="end" class="t-muted t-sm">output</text>

  <!-- axis titles -->
  <text x="296" y="334" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">layer (from output back to input)</text>
  <text x="26" y="170" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm" transform="rotate(-90 26 170)">gradient magnitude (log)</text>

  <!-- note -->
  <text x="280" y="362" text-anchor="middle" class="t-muted t-sm">Repeated multiplication by factors <1 shrinks the signal; factors >1 blow it up.</text>
</svg>

weight-init.caption: Each bar shows how spread-out a layer's outputs are as a signal passes deeper into the network. With weights **too small** the signal fades to nothing, with weights **too large** it blows up and clips, but **Xavier/He** initialization keeps the spread roughly the same in every layer so the network can actually learn.
weight-init.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 600 420" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Three rows of activation-spread bars across five layers comparing too-small, too-large, and Xavier/He initialization.">
  <!-- Title -->
  <text x="300" y="24" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg">Why good initialization matters</text>
  <text x="300" y="42" text-anchor="middle" class="t-muted t-sm">activation spread should stay stable across layers  L1 → L5</text>

  <!-- ===== Row 1: Too small ===== -->
  <rect x="14" y="58" width="572" height="92" rx="12" class="f-panel"/>
  <line x1="200" y1="105" x2="560" y2="105" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <!-- bars (centered on baseline = mean 0) -->
  <rect x="202" y="73"  width="36" height="64" class="f-muted s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="282" y="85"  width="36" height="40" class="f-muted s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="362" y="94"  width="36" height="22" class="f-muted s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="442" y="100" width="36" height="10" class="f-muted s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="522" y="103" width="36" height="4"  class="f-muted s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="188" y="101" text-anchor="end" class="t-bold t-muted">Too small init</text>
  <text x="188" y="117" text-anchor="end" class="t-muted t-sm">signal vanishes</text>

  <!-- ===== Row 2: Too large ===== -->
  <rect x="14" y="162" width="572" height="106" rx="12" class="f-panel"/>
  <line x1="200" y1="215" x2="560" y2="215" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <!-- saturation / clip limits -->
  <line x1="200" y1="181" x2="560" y2="181" class="s-bad" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5 4"/>
  <line x1="200" y1="249" x2="560" y2="249" class="s-bad" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5 4"/>
  <text x="556" y="176" text-anchor="end" class="t-bad t-sm">clip</text>
  <rect x="202" y="201" width="36" height="28" class="f-bad-soft s-bad" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="282" y="191" width="36" height="48" class="f-bad-soft s-bad" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="362" y="182" width="36" height="66" class="f-bad-soft s-bad" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="442" y="181" width="36" height="68" class="f-bad-soft s-bad" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="522" y="181" width="36" height="68" class="f-bad-soft s-bad" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="188" y="211" text-anchor="end" class="t-bold t-bad">Too large init</text>
  <text x="188" y="227" text-anchor="end" class="t-muted t-sm">saturates / clips</text>

  <!-- ===== Row 3: Xavier / He ===== -->
  <rect x="14" y="280" width="572" height="92" rx="12" class="f-panel"/>
  <line x1="200" y1="326" x2="560" y2="326" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="202" y="304" width="36" height="44" class="f-ok-soft s-ok" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="282" y="304" width="36" height="44" class="f-ok-soft s-ok" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="362" y="304" width="36" height="44" class="f-ok-soft s-ok" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="442" y="304" width="36" height="44" class="f-ok-soft s-ok" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="522" y="304" width="36" height="44" class="f-ok-soft s-ok" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="188" y="322" text-anchor="end" class="t-bold t-ok">Xavier / He init</text>
  <text x="188" y="338" text-anchor="end" class="t-muted t-sm">stable variance</text>

  <!-- Layer labels -->
  <text x="220" y="364" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">L1</text>
  <text x="300" y="364" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">L2</text>
  <text x="380" y="364" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">L3</text>
  <text x="460" y="364" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">L4</text>
  <text x="540" y="364" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">L5</text>

  <text x="300" y="411" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">deeper into the network</text>
</svg>

batchnorm.caption: Batch normalization first **re-centers and re-scales** each layer's output so the numbers have a tidy spread (subtract the mean $\mu$, divide by the standard deviation $\sigma$), then applies two **learned** knobs $\gamma$ and $\beta$ that let the network stretch and shift the result as needed: $y = \gamma\cdot(x-\mu)/\sigma + \beta$.
batchnorm.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 660 236" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Batch normalization block diagram: a layer output is normalized by subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation, then scaled by gamma and shifted by beta before going to the next layer.">
  <text x="330" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg">Batch Normalization</text>

  <!-- Input -->
  <rect x="18" y="80" width="86" height="52" rx="8" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="61" y="102" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">Layer</text>
  <text x="61" y="118" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">output</text>

  <line x1="104" y1="106" x2="127" y2="106" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- Normalize -->
  <rect x="130" y="70" width="180" height="72" rx="10" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text x="220" y="93" text-anchor="middle" class="t-accent t-bold">Normalize</text>
  <text x="220" y="111" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">subtract mean μ,</text>
  <text x="220" y="127" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">divide by std σ</text>

  <line x1="310" y1="106" x2="333" y2="106" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- Scale & shift -->
  <rect x="336" y="70" width="180" height="72" rx="10" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="426" y="93" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">Scale & shift</text>
  <text x="426" y="111" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">× γ ,  + β</text>
  <text x="426" y="127" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">(learned)</text>

  <line x1="516" y1="106" x2="539" y2="106" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- Output -->
  <rect x="542" y="80" width="100" height="52" rx="8" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="592" y="102" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">to next</text>
  <text x="592" y="118" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">layer</text>

  <!-- Formula -->
  <rect x="180" y="168" width="300" height="42" rx="8" class="f-panel2 s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="330" y="195" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">y = γ · (x - μ)/σ + β</text>
</svg>

norm-types.caption: Both methods rescale a grid of data where rows are **samples** and columns are **features**. **Batch Norm** normalizes down each column (one feature averaged across the whole batch), while **Layer Norm** normalizes across each row (one sample averaged over all of its features) — so Layer Norm treats every sample on its own.
norm-types.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 560 340" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Comparison of Batch Norm and Layer Norm on a grid of samples by features. Batch Norm highlights a column (one feature across all samples); Layer Norm highlights a row (one sample across all features).">
  <!-- Titles -->
  <text x="148" y="40" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-accent">Batch Norm</text>
  <text x="428" y="40" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-accent">Layer Norm</text>
  <text x="148" y="60" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">normalize each feature across the batch</text>
  <text x="428" y="60" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">normalize each sample across its features</text>

  <!-- ===== LEFT: Batch Norm grid ===== -->
  <rect x="80" y="80" width="136" height="136" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <!-- highlighted column (one feature) -->
  <rect x="114" y="80" width="34" height="136" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2"/>
  <!-- internal grid lines -->
  <line x1="114" y1="80" x2="114" y2="216" class="s-line" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="148" y1="80" x2="148" y2="216" class="s-line" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="182" y1="80" x2="182" y2="216" class="s-line" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="80" y1="114" x2="216" y2="114" class="s-line" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="80" y1="148" x2="216" y2="148" class="s-line" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="80" y1="182" x2="216" y2="182" class="s-line" stroke-width="1"/>

  <!-- axis labels left -->
  <text x="56" y="148" text-anchor="middle" transform="rotate(-90 56 148)" class="t-sm t-muted">samples ↓ (N)</text>
  <text x="148" y="240" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">features → (C)</text>
  <text x="148" y="270" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-bold">one column = one feature</text>

  <!-- ===== RIGHT: Layer Norm grid ===== -->
  <rect x="360" y="80" width="136" height="136" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <!-- highlighted row (one sample) -->
  <rect x="360" y="114" width="136" height="34" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2"/>
  <!-- internal grid lines -->
  <line x1="394" y1="80" x2="394" y2="216" class="s-line" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="428" y1="80" x2="428" y2="216" class="s-line" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="462" y1="80" x2="462" y2="216" class="s-line" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="360" y1="114" x2="496" y2="114" class="s-line" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="360" y1="148" x2="496" y2="148" class="s-line" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="360" y1="182" x2="496" y2="182" class="s-line" stroke-width="1"/>

  <!-- axis labels right -->
  <text x="340" y="148" text-anchor="middle" transform="rotate(-90 340 148)" class="t-sm t-muted">samples ↓ (N)</text>
  <text x="428" y="240" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">features → (C)</text>
  <text x="428" y="270" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-bold">one row = one sample</text>

  <!-- legend -->
  <rect x="196" y="306" width="16" height="14" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="220" y="318" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm t-muted">= the values averaged & scaled together</text>
</svg>

convolution-op.caption: A small **kernel** (here $3\times3$) slides over the input; at each spot it multiplies each overlapping number by the matching kernel weight and adds them up. That single sum, $1+2+0+1+1=5$, becomes **one cell** of the output **feature map**.
convolution-op.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 640 400" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Convolution: a 3 by 3 kernel multiplies an overlapping 3 by 3 window of the 5 by 5 input, and the products are summed to make one cell of the feature map.">
  <!-- ===== INPUT 5x5 ===== -->
  <text x="125" y="56" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">Input 5×5</text>
  <rect x="40" y="70" width="170" height="170" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <!-- window soft fill -->
  <rect x="40" y="70" width="102" height="102" class="f-accent-soft" stroke="none"/>
  <!-- grid lines -->
  <line x1="74" y1="70" x2="74" y2="240" class="s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="108" y1="70" x2="108" y2="240" class="s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="142" y1="70" x2="142" y2="240" class="s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="176" y1="70" x2="176" y2="240" class="s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="40" y1="104" x2="210" y2="104" class="s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="40" y1="138" x2="210" y2="138" class="s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="40" y1="172" x2="210" y2="172" class="s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="40" y1="206" x2="210" y2="206" class="s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <!-- window border highlight -->
  <rect x="40" y="70" width="102" height="102" fill="none" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <!-- window numbers -->
  <text x="57" y="92" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">1</text>
  <text x="91" y="92" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">0</text>
  <text x="125" y="92" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">2</text>
  <text x="57" y="126" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">3</text>
  <text x="91" y="126" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">1</text>
  <text x="125" y="126" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">0</text>
  <text x="57" y="160" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">0</text>
  <text x="91" y="160" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">2</text>
  <text x="125" y="160" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">1</text>
  <!-- other input numbers -->
  <text x="159" y="92" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">4</text>
  <text x="193" y="126" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">2</text>
  <text x="159" y="160" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">5</text>
  <text x="57" y="194" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">1</text>
  <text x="125" y="228" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">3</text>
  <text x="193" y="228" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">0</text>

  <!-- ===== KERNEL 3x3 ===== -->
  <text x="295" y="277" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold t-accent2">Kernel 3×3</text>
  <rect x="250" y="285" width="90" height="90" class="f-panel2 s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="280" y1="285" x2="280" y2="375" class="s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="310" y1="285" x2="310" y2="375" class="s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="250" y1="315" x2="340" y2="315" class="s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="250" y1="345" x2="340" y2="345" class="s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="265" y="305" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold t-accent2">1</text>
  <text x="295" y="305" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold t-accent2">0</text>
  <text x="325" y="305" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold t-accent2">1</text>
  <text x="265" y="335" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold t-accent2">0</text>
  <text x="295" y="335" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold t-accent2">1</text>
  <text x="325" y="335" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold t-accent2">0</text>
  <text x="265" y="365" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold t-accent2">1</text>
  <text x="295" y="365" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold t-accent2">0</text>
  <text x="325" y="365" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold t-accent2">1</text>

  <!-- ===== OPERATION ARROW ===== -->
  <text x="347" y="134" text-anchor="middle" class="t-accent t-sm">multiply overlapping cells</text>
  <line x1="220" y1="150" x2="476" y2="150" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <text x="347" y="172" text-anchor="middle" class="t-accent2 t-sm t-bold">sum of products</text>
  <text x="347" y="188" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">1 + 2 + 0 + 1 + 1 = 5</text>
  <text x="347" y="206" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">(window values × kernel weights)</text>

  <!-- ===== FEATURE MAP 3x3 ===== -->
  <text x="540" y="116" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">Feature map</text>
  <rect x="480" y="130" width="120" height="120" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <!-- highlighted output cell -->
  <rect x="480" y="130" width="40" height="40" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <line x1="520" y1="130" x2="520" y2="250" class="s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="560" y1="130" x2="560" y2="250" class="s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="480" y1="170" x2="600" y2="170" class="s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="480" y1="210" x2="600" y2="210" class="s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="500" y="157" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-accent t-bold">5</text>
  <text x="540" y="270" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">= one output cell</text>
</svg>

padding-stride.caption: **Padding** wraps the input in a border of zeros so the output stays the same size, while a **stride of 2** makes the sliding window jump two cells at a time, skipping positions and producing a smaller output.

receptive-field.caption: Stacking convolution layers makes each neuron "see" more of the original input: the highlighted **top neuron** connects to just **3 neurons** below it, but those in turn reach back to **5 input cells**. With more layers this window keeps growing, so deep neurons respond to large patterns while shallow ones only see tiny patches.
receptive-field.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 600 372" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="A three-layer 1D convolutional network: one top neuron connects through three middle-layer neurons down to five input cells, showing how the receptive field grows with depth.">
  <text x="16" y="26" class="t-lg">Growing receptive field</text>
  <text x="584" y="26" text-anchor="end" class="t-sm t-muted">kernel = 3</text>

  <!-- deep neuron tag -->
  <text x="320" y="44" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent">deep neuron</text>
  <line x1="320" y1="46" x2="320" y2="51" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>

  <!-- row labels -->
  <text x="108" y="73" text-anchor="end" class="t-sm t-muted">Conv 2 (5)</text>
  <text x="108" y="179" text-anchor="end" class="t-sm t-muted">Conv 1 (7)</text>
  <text x="108" y="285" text-anchor="end" class="t-sm t-muted">Input (9)</text>

  <!-- conv1 -> input fan (drawn first, behind cells' overlap is fine) -->
  <line x1="278" y1="192" x2="236" y2="264" class="s-accent" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="278" y1="192" x2="278" y2="264" class="s-accent" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="278" y1="192" x2="320" y2="264" class="s-accent" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="320" y1="192" x2="278" y2="264" class="s-accent" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="320" y1="192" x2="320" y2="264" class="s-accent" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="320" y1="192" x2="362" y2="264" class="s-accent" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="362" y1="192" x2="320" y2="264" class="s-accent" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="362" y1="192" x2="362" y2="264" class="s-accent" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="362" y1="192" x2="404" y2="264" class="s-accent" stroke-width="1.5"/>

  <!-- conv2 -> conv1 fan -->
  <line x1="320" y1="86" x2="278" y2="158" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2.2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <line x1="320" y1="86" x2="320" y2="158" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2.2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <line x1="320" y1="86" x2="362" y2="158" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2.2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>

  <!-- Conv 2 row -->
  <rect x="219" y="52" width="34" height="34" rx="4" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="261" y="52" width="34" height="34" rx="4" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="303" y="52" width="34" height="34" rx="4" class="f-accent s-accent" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <rect x="345" y="52" width="34" height="34" rx="4" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="387" y="52" width="34" height="34" rx="4" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>

  <!-- Conv 1 row -->
  <rect x="177" y="158" width="34" height="34" rx="4" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>

pooling.caption: **Max pooling** slides a non-overlapping $2\times2$ window (stride $2$) across the grid and keeps only the largest value in each block, shrinking a $4\times4$ map down to $2\times2$. The circled number in each colored block is the one that survives and lands in the matching output cell.
pooling.svg:

conv-feature-maps.caption: Each **filter** is a small grid of weights that slides over the image and lights up wherever it spots a particular pattern, such as **edges**, **corners**, or **texture**. Every filter outputs its own **feature map**, so a single input image turns into many stacked channels.

cnn-timeline.caption: A timeline of landmark CNN designs: each **bar grows taller** as networks get deeper, jumping from a handful of layers in $LeNet$ (1998) to **152 layers** in $ResNet$ (2015). At the same time the green arrow shows their image-recognition mistakes steadily shrinking, from roughly $26\%$ down to about $3.6\%$.
cnn-timeline.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 640 380" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Timeline of CNN architectures from LeNet-5 (1998) to ResNet (2015), with bars showing network depth growing from a few layers to 152, and an arrow noting ImageNet error falling.">
  <!-- title -->
  <text x="320" y="26" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg">Evolution of CNN Architectures</text>

  <!-- legend -->
  <rect x="58" y="42" width="16" height="11" rx="2" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="80" y="51" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm t-muted">bar height = network depth (layers)</text>

  <!-- ImageNet error annotation -->

residual-block.caption: A **residual block** lets the input $x$ skip past the two weight layers (the **highlighted "identity" arrow**) and get added back at the **+** node, so the layers only need to learn the leftover change $F(x)$ — making **output $= F(x) + x$**. This shortcut makes very deep networks much easier to train.
residual-block.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 420 460" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Residual block: input x flows through two weight layers, then x is added back via an identity skip connection before a final ReLU.">
  <!-- input x -->
  <text x="160" y="42" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-bold">x</text>
  <line x1="160" y1="50" x2="160" y2="78" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- weight layer + ReLU -->
  <rect x="70" y="80" width="180" height="54" rx="8" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="160" y="104" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">weight layer</text>
  <text x="160" y="122" text-anchor="middle" class="t-muted t-sm">+ ReLU</text>
  <line x1="160" y1="134" x2="160" y2="162" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- weight layer -->
  <rect x="70" y="164" width="180" height="48" rx="8" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="160" y="193" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">weight layer</text>

  <!-- F(x) label on main path -->
  <text x="172" y="240" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm t-muted">F(x)</text>
  <line x1="160" y1="212" x2="160" y2="258" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- plus node -->
  <circle cx="160" cy="278" r="18" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="160" y="284" text-anchor="middle" class="t-accent t-lg t-bold">+</text>

  <!-- skip / identity curved arrow -->
  <path d="M 160 56 C 320 56 340 200 340 220 C 340 250 300 278 182 278"
        class="s-accent" stroke-width="2.5" fill="none" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <text x="350" y="160" text-anchor="middle" class="t-accent t-bold" transform="rotate(90 350 160)">identity x</text>
  <text x="350" y="178" text-anchor="middle" class="t-accent t-sm" transform="rotate(90 350 178)">(skip)</text>

  <!-- after plus to ReLU -->
  <line x1="160" y1="296" x2="160" y2="324" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <rect x="100" y="326" width="120" height="44" rx="8" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="160" y="353" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">ReLU</text>

  <!-- output -->
  <line x1="160" y1="370" x2="160" y2="398" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <text x="160" y="418" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-bold">output</text>

  <!-- note -->
  <text x="210" y="448" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">learn the residual F(x);  output = F(x) + x</text>
</svg>

inception-module.caption: An **Inception module** runs the same input through four paths at once — a $1\times1$ filter, a $3\times3$ and a $5\times5$ filter (each first squeezed by a cheap $1\times1$ step), and a pooling path — then stacks all their outputs together. This lets the network **see patterns at several sizes in one layer** instead of picking just one.
inception-module.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 660 452" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Inception module: an input feature map fans up into four parallel branches (1x1 conv; 1x1 then 3x3; 1x1 then 5x5; 3x3 max pool then 1x1) that all feed into one concatenate bar and an output.">
  <!-- Output -->
  <rect class="f-panel s-line" x="275" y="16" width="110" height="30" rx="5" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="330" y="36" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">Output</text>
  <line class="s-accent" x1="330" y1="60" x2="330" y2="46" stroke-width="2.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>

  <!-- Concatenate bar -->
  <rect class="f-accent-soft s-accent" x="35" y="60" width="590" height="34" rx="6" stroke-width="2.5"/>

plain-vs-resnet.caption: The **degradation problem**: stacking more layers on a *plain* network can make it train **worse** -- the 56-layer plain net ends with higher error than the shallower 20-layer one. A **ResNet** of the same depth, using **skip connections**, trains to the lowest error of all.
plain-vs-resnet.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 520 372" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Training error versus iterations: a 56-layer plain network trains worse than a 20-layer plain network, while a 56-layer ResNet trains best.">
  <!-- axes -->
  <line class="s-line" x1="70" y1="300" x2="70" y2="38" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- axis labels -->

  <text class="t-sm t-muted" x="30" y="170" text-anchor="middle" transform="rotate(-90 30 170)">training error</text>

  <!-- curves -->

  <text class="t-sm" x="352" y="110">plain-20</text>

  <line class="s-ok" x1="314" y1="126" x2="344" y2="126" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text class="t-sm" x="352" y="130">ResNet-56 (best)</text>
