  <text class="t-sm t-accent" x="277" y="358" text-anchor="middle">Skip connections let very deep nets train.</text>
</svg>

one-by-one-conv.caption: A **1×1 convolution** looks at just one pixel at a time and blends together all of its channel values, turning a tall stack of $6$ input channels into a shorter stack of $3$ output channels. It is an inexpensive way to **mix or shrink the depth** of a feature map without changing its width or height.
one-by-one-conv.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 560 330" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="A 1 by 1 convolution takes the stack of 6 input channels at one pixel, mixes them, and produces a shorter stack of 3 output channels at the same pixel.">
  <text x="280" y="36" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-bold">1×1 Convolution</text>

  <!-- input stack label -->
  <text x="105" y="66" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">input: 6 channels</text>

  <!-- input channel cells (one pixel, all channels) -->
  <rect x="80" y="78"  width="50" height="30" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="80" y="108" width="50" height="30" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="80" y="138" width="50" height="30" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="80" y="168" width="50" height="30" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="80" y="198" width="50" height="30" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="80" y="228" width="50" height="30" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="105" y="278" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">(one spatial pixel)</text>

  <!-- connectors: input channels converge into the conv -->
  <line x1="130" y1="93"  x2="261" y2="170" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <line x1="130" y1="123" x2="261" y2="170" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <line x1="130" y1="153" x2="261" y2="170" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <line x1="130" y1="183" x2="261" y2="170" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <line x1="130" y1="213" x2="261" y2="170" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <line x1="130" y1="243" x2="261" y2="170" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.2"/>

  <!-- 1x1 conv node -->
  <rect x="261" y="150" width="58" height="40" rx="6" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text x="290" y="175" text-anchor="middle" class="t-accent t-bold">1×1</text>
  <text x="290" y="212" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent">conv (mixes depth)</text>

  <!-- connectors: conv produces each output channel -->
  <line x1="319" y1="170" x2="428" y2="140" class="s-accent" stroke-width="1.8" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <line x1="319" y1="170" x2="428" y2="170" class="s-accent" stroke-width="1.8" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <line x1="319" y1="170" x2="428" y2="200" class="s-accent" stroke-width="1.8" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>

  <!-- output stack label -->
  <text x="455" y="112" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">output: 3 channels</text>

  <!-- output channel cells -->
  <rect x="430" y="125" width="50" height="30" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="430" y="155" width="50" height="30" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="430" y="185" width="50" height="30" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="1.5"/>

  <!-- depth change note -->
  <text x="455" y="238" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent">depth 6 → 3</text>

  <!-- bottom caption -->
  <text x="280" y="312" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">Mixes / reduces channels at each pixel — a cheap way to change depth.</text>
</svg>

transfer-learning.caption: **Transfer learning** keeps the early layers of a network that already learned general image features on a huge dataset (ImageNet) **frozen** (locked, not retrained), and only swaps in and trains a small **new head** on your own task. This lets you get strong results even with a small dataset and little compute.
transfer-learning.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 600 300" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Transfer learning: early network layers are frozen and reused from an ImageNet-pretrained model, while the last block is replaced with a new trainable head for your own task.">
  <!-- top adapt arrow + labels -->
  <text x="72" y="38" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm t-muted">pretrained on ImageNet</text>
  <text x="528" y="38" text-anchor="end" class="t-sm t-accent">adapt to your small dataset</text>

feature-extract-vs-finetune.caption: Both setups reuse a **pretrained backbone**, but with **feature extraction** every backbone block stays locked and only the new classifier head learns, while **fine-tuning** also unlocks the top blocks so they adjust to your data. Locked blocks keep their original weights; highlighted blocks are the ones actually being trained.

rnn-cell.caption: A **recurrent (RNN) cell**: at each step it combines the current input $x_t$ with the previous state $h_{t-1}$ to produce a new state $h_t$. The curved arrow is the **memory loop** — that new state is fed back in as the input state for the next step.
rnn-cell.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 480 410" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="A recurrent RNN cell taking input x_t and previous state h_{t-1}, producing new state h_t with a self-loop feeding the state back in.">
  <!-- formula header -->
  <rect x="84" y="18" width="312" height="30" rx="8" class="f-panel s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="240" y="38" text-anchor="middle">h_t = tanh(W·x_t + U·h_{t-1} + b)</text>

  <!-- output h_t -->
  <text x="240" y="76" text-anchor="middle" class="t-accent t-lg">h_t</text>
  <line x1="240" y1="148" x2="240" y2="86" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <text x="250" y="112" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm t-muted">new state</text>

  <!-- recurrent self-loop: out the right, around the bottom, back into the left -->
  <path d="M300 195 C372 215 372 360 240 360 C108 360 108 215 180 195" fill="none" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <text x="240" y="384" text-anchor="middle" class="t-accent">h_{t-1}  (previous state)</text>

  <!-- cell -->

  <text x="240" y="187" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold t-lg">RNN</text>

</svg>

rnn-unrolled.caption: An RNN drawn **unrolled**: the same cell is copied once per time step, reading an input $x_t$ and producing an output $y_t$, while passing a memory called the **hidden state** $h_t$ along to the next step. Crucially, every copy shares the *same weights* — it is one network reused over time, not three separate ones.
rnn-unrolled.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 640 320" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="A recurrent neural network unrolled across three time steps t-1, t and t+1. Each cell takes an input x from below, emits an output y above, and passes a hidden state h to the next cell; the same weights are reused every step.">
  <!-- ===== hidden-state recurrence arrows (left to right) ===== -->
  <!-- dashed continuation from the past -->
  <line x1="64" y1="155" x2="110" y2="155" class="s-muted" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="4 3" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <text x="400" y="147" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent">h_t</text>

  <!-- dashed continuation to the future -->

  <text x="590" y="159" text-anchor="middle" class="t-muted t-sm">···</text>

  <!-- ===== CELL t-1 ===== -->
  <line x1="160" y1="120" x2="160" y2="60" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <text x="160" y="50" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">y_t-1</text>

  <!-- ===== CELL t ===== -->

  <text x="320" y="171" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">step t</text>

  <!-- ===== CELL t+1 ===== -->

</svg>

bptt.caption: To learn from a sequence, an RNN is "unrolled" into a chain of cells and the error at the **Loss** is sent backward, step by step, from right to left. Because the gradient is **multiplied** again at every step, over many steps it tends to shrink toward zero (**vanish**) or blow up (**explode**), which makes long-range learning hard.

  <!-- legend -->

  <!-- forward hidden-state arrows -->

rnn-task-types.caption: Recurrent networks come in different **input-to-output shapes**: **one-to-one** (one input, one answer, like ordinary classification), **one-to-many** (one input spins out a sequence, like captioning an image), **many-to-one** (a whole sequence gives a single verdict, like rating a review's sentiment), and **many-to-many** (a sequence in, a sequence out, like translation). The orange arrows in the middle row are the recurrent links that pass memory from one step to the next.

lstm-cell.caption: An **LSTM cell** carries a running **memory** along the bold top line ($C_{t-1}\to C_t$). Small **gates** decide what to erase (forget $\times$), what new information to write (input $+$ candidate), and what to reveal as the output $h_t$ — each $\sigma$ gate outputs a value between 0 and 1, acting like a soft on/off dial.
lstm-cell.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 660 448" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Inside an LSTM cell: a bold cell-state memory line across the top passes through a forget multiply and an add node; forget, input, output sigmoid gates and a candidate tanh sit below fed by h_{t-1} and x_t; output h_t leaves the top right via tanh of the cell state.">
  <!-- legend (top-left margin) -->
  <text x="24" y="26" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm t-muted">σ = sigmoid gate (0..1)</text>
  <text x="24" y="42" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm t-muted">× , +  pointwise ops</text>

  <!-- container -->
  <rect x="70" y="46" width="520" height="330" rx="22" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>

  <!-- ===== cell-state memory highway ===== -->
  <line x1="24" y1="86" x2="636" y2="86" class="s-accent" stroke-width="3.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <text x="24" y="76" text-anchor="start" class="t-accent t-bold t-sm">C_{t-1}</text>
  <text x="628" y="76" text-anchor="end" class="t-accent t-bold t-sm">C_t</text>
  <text x="250" y="73" text-anchor="middle" class="t-muted t-sm">cell state · memory</text>

  <!-- ===== connectors up into highway nodes ===== -->
  <!-- forget gate -> forget multiply -->

gru-cell.caption: A **GRU cell** updates its memory with just two gates: the **reset gate $r$** decides how much of the old memory to forget when proposing a fresh **candidate** ($\tanh$), and the **update gate $z$** decides the mix — the new state is a blend $h_t=(1-z)\cdot h_{t-1}+z\cdot \tilde h$ of old memory and new candidate. It is simpler than an LSTM but usually works just as well.
gru-cell.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 600 446" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Inside a GRU cell showing update gate z, reset gate r, candidate h-tilde, and a blend producing h_t">
  <text x="300" y="30" class="t-lg" text-anchor="middle">Inside a GRU cell</text>

  <!-- container -->
  <rect x="54" y="48" width="492" height="352" rx="20" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>

  <!-- equation panel -->
  <rect x="160" y="62" width="280" height="32" rx="9" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="300" y="83" class="t-sm t-bold" text-anchor="middle">h_t = (1-z)·h_{t-1} + z·h~</text>

  <!-- state highway in -->
  <line x1="14" y1="140" x2="54" y2="140" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <text x="32" y="130" class="t-sm t-bold" text-anchor="middle">h_{t-1}</text>

  <line x1="54" y1="140" x2="382" y2="140" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <circle cx="96" cy="140" r="4" class="f-accent s-accent" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="232" y="130" class="t-sm t-muted" text-anchor="middle">(1-z)·h_{t-1}</text>

  <!-- blend node -->
  <circle cx="400" cy="140" r="18" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text x="400" y="146" class="t-lg t-accent" text-anchor="middle">+</text>

  <!-- state highway out -->
  <line x1="418" y1="140" x2="546" y2="140" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <line x1="546" y1="140" x2="584" y2="140" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <text x="566" y="130" class="t-sm t-bold t-accent" text-anchor="middle">h_t</text>

  <!-- gate / candidate boxes -->
  <rect x="101" y="240" width="98" height="56" rx="10" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="150" y="266" class="t-lg t-accent" text-anchor="middle">σ</text>
  <text x="150" y="285" class="t-sm" text-anchor="middle">reset r</text>

  <rect x="273" y="240" width="98" height="56" rx="10" class="f-ok-soft s-ok" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="322" y="265" class="t-bold t-ok" text-anchor="middle">tanh</text>
  <text x="322" y="285" class="t-sm" text-anchor="middle">candidate h~</text>

  <rect x="421" y="240" width="98" height="56" rx="10" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="470" y="266" class="t-lg t-accent" text-anchor="middle">σ</text>
  <text x="470" y="285" class="t-sm" text-anchor="middle">update z</text>

  <!-- reset feeds candidate -->
  <line x1="199" y1="268" x2="271" y2="268" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <text x="235" y="259" class="t-sm t-accent" text-anchor="middle">r</text>

  <!-- candidate feeds blend -->
  <polyline points="322,240 322,182 388,152" class="s-ok" fill="none" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-ok)"/>
  <text x="300" y="210" class="t-sm t-ok" text-anchor="middle">z·h~</text>

  <!-- update gate controls blend -->
  <polyline points="470,240 470,184 412,152" class="s-accent" fill="none" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <text x="488" y="208" class="t-sm t-accent" text-anchor="middle">z</text>

  <!-- input bus -->
  <line x1="96" y1="352" x2="470" y2="352" class="s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <line x1="96" y1="140" x2="96" y2="352" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2"/>
  <line x1="150" y1="352" x2="150" y2="298" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="322" y1="352" x2="322" y2="298" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="470" y1="352" x2="470" y2="298" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- x_t input -->
  <line x1="250" y1="382" x2="250" y2="354" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <text x="250" y="394" class="t-sm t-bold" text-anchor="middle">x_t</text>

  <!-- note -->
  <text x="300" y="426" class="t-sm t-muted" text-anchor="middle">Fewer gates than LSTM - often just as good.</text>
</svg>

cell-state-highway.caption: The LSTM **cell state** $C$ is like a highway running straight across time: at each step it is only lightly edited by a multiply (the forget gate, $\times$) and an add (new input, $+$), with **no squashing** in between. Because nothing repeatedly shrinks the signal, **gradients can flow backward almost unchanged** over many steps — unlike a vanilla RNN, which squashes its state every step and lets gradients vanish.
cell-state-highway.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 640 340" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="The LSTM cell state acts as a gradient highway across time steps, edited only by a multiply (forget gate) and an add (input) at each step, with no squashing nonlinearity, so gradients flow backward almost unchanged, unlike a vanilla RNN that squashes the state every step.">
  <!-- Title -->
  <text x="320" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg">The Gradient Highway</text>
  <text x="320" y="50" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">× scales by forget gate · + adds new input — the only edits on the path</text>

  <!-- Feed labels (gate signals entering each operator) -->
  <text x="150" y="76" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent2">f_t</text>
  <text x="240" y="76" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-ok">i_t</text>
  <text x="360" y="76" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent2">f_(t+1)</text>
  <text x="450" y="76" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-ok">i_(t+1)</text>

  <!-- Feed arrows into operators -->
  <line x1="150" y1="84" x2="150" y2="102" class="s-line" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="360" y1="84" x2="360" y2="102" class="s-line" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="240" y1="84" x2="240" y2="102" class="s-ok" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-ok)"/>
  <line x1="450" y1="84" x2="450" y2="102" class="s-ok" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-ok)"/>

  <!-- "no squash" note on the path -->
  <text x="300" y="100" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-bad">no tanh on this path</text>

  <!-- The cell-state highway (forward in time) -->
  <line x1="50" y1="120" x2="600" y2="120" class="s-accent" stroke-width="5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>

  <!-- Forget (×) operators -->
  <circle cx="150" cy="120" r="16" class="f-accent-soft s-accent2" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="150" y="126" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-bold">×</text>
  <circle cx="360" cy="120" r="16" class="f-accent-soft s-accent2" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="360" y="126" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-bold">×</text>

  <!-- Input (+) operators -->
  <circle cx="240" cy="120" r="16" class="f-ok-soft s-ok" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="240" y="126" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-bold">+</text>
  <circle cx="450" cy="120" r="16" class="f-ok-soft s-ok" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="450" y="126" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-bold">+</text>

  <!-- Cell-state labels along the line -->
  <text x="100" y="146" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent">C_(t-1)</text>
  <text x="300" y="146" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent">C_t</text>
  <text x="500" y="146" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent">C_(t+1)</text>

  <!-- Gradient flows backward (green) -->
  <line x1="580" y1="178" x2="70" y2="178" class="s-ok" stroke-width="3" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-ok)"/>
  <text x="320" y="198" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-ok">gradient flows back almost unchanged</text>

  <!-- Contrast panel -->
  <rect x="40" y="220" width="560" height="104" rx="10" class="f-panel s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="58" y="244" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm t-bold">Why the highway matters</text>
  <text x="58" y="272" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm t-bad t-bold">Vanilla RNN:</text>
  <text x="138" y="272" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm">tanh squashes C every step → gradient shrinks & vanishes</text>
  <text x="58" y="300" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm t-ok t-bold">LSTM:</text>
  <text x="104" y="300" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm">additive cell-state path stays open → gradient survives many steps</text>
</svg>

bidirectional-rnn.caption: A **bidirectional RNN** reads the same sequence twice: a forward pass (blue, left to right) builds up **past** context, while a backward pass (orange, right to left) builds up **future** context. At each position the two hidden states are joined together, so every output $y_t = [\,h_t^{\rightarrow}\,;\,h_t^{\leftarrow}\,]$ is informed by the **whole** sequence, not just what came before.
bidirectional-rnn.svg:

  <text x="580" y="383" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">x4</text>

  <!-- ===== legend ===== -->
  <line x1="60" y1="430" x2="88" y2="430" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <text x="94" y="434" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm t-accent">h→ forward = PAST</text>
  <line x1="384" y1="430" x2="356" y2="430" class="s-accent2" stroke-width="2.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <text x="390" y="434" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm t-accent2">h← backward = FUTURE</text>
</svg>

seq2seq.caption: An encoder-decoder (**seq2seq**) model. The **encoder** reads the source words one by one and squeezes the entire sentence into a single **context vector $c$** — the *bottleneck*. The **decoder** then uses $c$ (plus the word it just produced) to generate the translation one word at a time.

attention-mechanism.caption: **Attention** lets the decoder peek at every input word at once instead of relying on a single summary. Each encoder state $h_i$ gets a weight $\alpha_i$ (the thick accent line is the most-attended input), and since the weights add up to $1$ the decoder builds its **context** as a weighted blend $context_t=\sum_i \alpha_i h_i$ that it then reads from.
attention-mechanism.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 640 410" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Attention mechanism: a decoder state s_t attends to four encoder hidden states h1..h4 with weights alpha1..alpha4 that sum to 1; the weighted sum forms the context vector that feeds the decoder.">
  <!-- title note -->
  <text x="320" y="24" class="t-sm t-muted" text-anchor="middle">Attention: the decoder looks at all inputs, weighting the relevant ones</text>

  <!-- decoder state s_t (top right) -->
  <rect x="500" y="50" width="124" height="50" rx="9" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="562" y="73" class="t-bold" text-anchor="middle">s_t</text>
  <text x="562" y="89" class="t-sm t-muted" text-anchor="middle">(decoder state)</text>

  <!-- attention weighted lines: s_t -> each h_i (thickness = weight) -->
  <line x1="500" y1="78" x2="80"  y2="176" class="s-muted"  stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="500" y1="78" x2="180" y2="176" class="s-line"   stroke-width="3"   marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="500" y1="78" x2="280" y2="176" class="s-accent" stroke-width="7"   marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <line x1="500" y1="78" x2="380" y2="176" class="s-muted"  stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- weight labels above each encoder box -->
  <text x="80"  y="162" class="t-sm t-muted" text-anchor="middle">α1=0.1</text>
  <text x="180" y="162" class="t-sm t-muted" text-anchor="middle">α2=0.2</text>
  <text x="280" y="160" class="t-accent t-bold" text-anchor="middle">α3=0.6</text>
  <text x="380" y="162" class="t-sm t-muted" text-anchor="middle">α4=0.1</text>

  <!-- encoder hidden states (bottom row) -->
  <rect x="48"  y="176" width="64" height="48" rx="6" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <rect x="148" y="176" width="64" height="48" rx="6" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <rect x="248" y="176" width="64" height="48" rx="6" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2"/>
  <rect x="348" y="176" width="64" height="48" rx="6" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="80"  y="206" class="t-bold" text-anchor="middle">h1</text>
  <text x="180" y="206" class="t-bold" text-anchor="middle">h2</text>
  <text x="280" y="206" class="t-bold" text-anchor="middle">h3</text>
  <text x="380" y="206" class="t-bold" text-anchor="middle">h4</text>

  <!-- combine arrows: h_i -> context -->
  <line x1="80"  y1="224" x2="220" y2="294" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="180" y1="224" x2="280" y2="294" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="280" y1="224" x2="340" y2="294" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="380" y1="224" x2="400" y2="294" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- context vector box -->
  <rect x="160" y="296" width="320" height="50" rx="8" class="f-panel2 s-accent" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text x="320" y="319" class="t-bold" text-anchor="middle">context_t = Σ αi · hi</text>
  <text x="320" y="337" class="t-sm t-muted" text-anchor="middle">weighted sum of all hidden states</text>

  <!-- feed-to-decoder arrow -->
  <line x1="480" y1="321" x2="560" y2="101" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <text x="600" y="232" class="t-sm t-accent" text-anchor="end">feeds decoder</text>

  <!-- footnotes -->
  <text x="320" y="368" class="t-sm t-muted" text-anchor="middle">α1 + α2 + α3 + α4 = 1</text>
  <text x="18"  y="372" class="t-sm t-muted" text-anchor="start">h1..h4 = encoder hidden states</text>
  <text x="18"  y="390" class="t-sm t-muted" text-anchor="start">thicker line = more attention</text>
</svg>

attention-heatmap.caption: This **attention heatmap** shows how strongly each output word ("the agreement was signed") looks at each input word ("l'accord a ete signe") while translating. The bright cells form a near-diagonal path, meaning each word mostly attends to its counterpart, and **darker means more attention**.

self-attention-qkv.caption: Self-attention lets the word **cat** gather context: its **query** $q$ is compared (dot product) with every word's **key** $k_i$ to get scores, **softmax** turns those into weights that add up to 1, and the new vector for *cat* is a weighted blend of every word's **value** $v_i$ — here mostly *cat* and *sat*. The key point is that $Q$, $K$ and $V$ all come from the **same sentence**.
self-attention-qkv.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 660 350" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Self-attention: the query vector q of the token cat is compared with each token's key to produce scores, softmax turns scores into weights, and a weighted sum of the value vectors gives the new representation of cat.">
  <!-- Title -->
  <text x="330" y="20" class="t-lg" text-anchor="middle">Self-attention with Q, K, V</text>
  <text x="330" y="37" class="t-sm t-muted" text-anchor="middle">Q, K and V are linear projections of the SAME token sequence</text>

  <!-- Token row -->
  <rect x="146" y="52" width="80" height="32" rx="6" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>

  <text x="282" y="99" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent">k2, v2, q</text>
  <text x="378" y="99" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">k3, v3</text>
  <text x="474" y="99" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">k4, v4</text>

  <!-- column headers -->
  <text x="74"  y="117" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent">Query (Q)</text>
  <text x="221" y="117" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">scores · Keys (K)</text>

  <line x1="108" y1="206" x2="174" y2="184" class="s-accent" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>

  <text x="221" y="232" text-anchor="middle">q · k3</text>

  <!-- scores -> softmax -->

  <line x1="267" y1="184" x2="286" y2="184" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <line x1="267" y1="272" x2="286" y2="272" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- softmax panel -->
  <rect x="287" y="125" width="42" height="162" rx="8" class="f-panel2 s-muted" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="308" y="206" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold" transform="rotate(-90 308 206)">softmax</text>

  <!-- softmax -> weights -->
  <line x1="329" y1="140" x2="349" y2="140" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="329" y1="184" x2="349" y2="184" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="329" y1="228" x2="349" y2="228" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="329" y1="272" x2="349" y2="272" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- weight boxes (sum to 1) -->
  <rect x="350" y="125" width="56" height="30" rx="5" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <rect x="350" y="169" width="56" height="30" rx="5" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2"/>
  <rect x="350" y="213" width="56" height="30" rx="5" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <rect x="350" y="257" width="56" height="30" rx="5" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="378" y="144" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">a1=.10</text>
  <text x="378" y="188" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-bold">a2=.55</text>
  <text x="378" y="232" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">a3=.25</text>
  <text x="378" y="276" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">a4=.10</text>

  <!-- multiply operator links a_i x v_i -->
  <line x1="406" y1="140" x2="433" y2="140" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="406" y1="184" x2="433" y2="184" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="406" y1="228" x2="433" y2="228" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="406" y1="272" x2="433" y2="272" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="420" y="135" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-bold">×</text>
  <text x="420" y="179" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-bold">×</text>
  <text x="420" y="223" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-bold">×</text>
  <text x="420" y="267" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-bold">×</text>

  <!-- value boxes -->
  <rect x="434" y="125" width="56" height="30" rx="5" class="f-panel2 s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <rect x="434" y="169" width="56" height="30" rx="5" class="f-panel2 s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <rect x="434" y="213" width="56" height="30" rx="5" class="f-panel2 s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <rect x="434" y="257" width="56" height="30" rx="5" class="f-panel2 s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="462" y="144" text-anchor="middle">v1</text>
  <text x="462" y="188" text-anchor="middle">v2</text>
  <text x="462" y="232" text-anchor="middle">v3</text>
  <text x="462" y="276" text-anchor="middle">v4</text>

  <!-- values -> output (weighted sum) -->
  <line x1="490" y1="140" x2="519" y2="170" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="490" y1="184" x2="519" y2="196" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="490" y1="228" x2="519" y2="216" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="490" y1="272" x2="519" y2="242" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- output box -->

  <text x="574" y="200" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold t-accent">new cat</text>
  <text x="574" y="220" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">= Σ a_i · v_i</text>

  <text x="221" y="312" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">(1) scores = q · k_i</text>

  <text x="556" y="312" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">(3) Σ weight × value</text>
</svg>

scaled-dot-product.caption: Attention compares each query against every key to get **similarity scores**, shrinks them by $\sqrt{d_k}$ so they stay stable, runs **softmax** to turn them into weights that add up to 1, and uses those weights to blend the values $V$ into the output.
scaled-dot-product.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 620 384" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Scaled dot-product attention as a vertical pipeline: Q dot K transpose for similarity scores, divide by sqrt of d_k to scale, softmax to make weights, multiply by V for a weighted sum, then output. A side panel shows the compact formula.">
  <!-- Pipeline boxes -->
  <rect x="70" y="28" width="220" height="50" rx="8" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="180" y="50" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">Q · K^T</text>
  <text x="180" y="68" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">similarity scores</text>

  <rect x="70" y="100" width="220" height="50" rx="8" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="180" y="122" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">÷ sqrt(d_k)</text>
  <text x="180" y="140" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">scale</text>

  <rect x="70" y="172" width="220" height="50" rx="8" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text x="180" y="194" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold t-accent">softmax</text>
  <text x="180" y="212" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">turn into weights</text>

  <rect x="70" y="244" width="220" height="50" rx="8" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="180" y="266" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">× V</text>
  <text x="180" y="284" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">weighted sum</text>

  <rect x="70" y="316" width="220" height="40" rx="8" class="f-ok-soft s-ok" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="180" y="341" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold t-ok">output</text>

  <!-- Connectors -->
  <line x1="180" y1="78" x2="180" y2="97" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="180" y1="150" x2="180" y2="169" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="180" y1="222" x2="180" y2="241" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="180" y1="294" x2="180" y2="313" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- Formula panel -->
  <rect x="326" y="150" width="282" height="84" rx="10" class="f-panel2 s-muted" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="467" y="174" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">Compact form</text>
  <text x="467" y="198" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">Attention(Q, K, V) =</text>
  <text x="467" y="221" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold t-accent2">softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k)) · V</text>
</svg>

multi-head-attention.caption: Multi-head attention runs several attention "heads" in parallel, and **each head learns a different kind of relationship** in the input. Their outputs are then **concatenated** and passed through a single **linear layer** to produce the final result.
multi-head-attention.svg:

  <!-- split bus -> heads -->

positional-encoding.caption: Because self-attention looks at all words at once with **no built-in notion of order**, the model would otherwise treat a sentence as an unordered bag of words. To fix this, each word's embedding $e_i$ is **added** to a position vector $p_i$ — a unique sinusoidal pattern (different wiggle for each slot) — so the combined input $e_i + p_i$ tells the transformer both *what* the word is and *where* it sits.
positional-encoding.svg:

  <line x1="535" y1="64" x2="535" y2="42" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>

transformer-block.caption: A transformer encoder block stacks two main stages: **multi-head self-attention** (each token looks at the others) and a small **feed-forward network**, each followed by an *Add & Norm* step. The orange **skip** arrows are shortcuts that let the original signal bypass a stage and get added back in, which keeps training stable.
transformer-block.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 440 460" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Transformer encoder block: input flows up through multi-head self-attention, add and norm, feed-forward, and add and norm to output, with two residual skip connections.">
  <!-- title -->
  <text x="220" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-bold">Transformer Encoder Block</text>

  <!-- outer block -->
  <rect x="80" y="42" width="280" height="404" rx="10" class="f-none s-muted" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="7 5"/>

  <!-- connectors (bottom to top), center x=220 -->
  <line x1="220" y1="406" x2="220" y2="340" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="220" y1="266" x2="220" y2="252" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="220" y1="220" x2="220" y2="206" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="220" y1="132" x2="220" y2="118" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <line x1="220" y1="86" x2="220" y2="76" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- skip / residual arrows -->
  <path d="M 220 392 C 98 392, 98 237, 145 235" fill="none" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <path d="M 220 212 C 98 212, 98 103, 145 101" fill="none" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <circle cx="220" cy="392" r="3.5" class="f-accent"/>
  <circle cx="220" cy="212" r="3.5" class="f-accent"/>
  <text x="126" y="312" text-anchor="middle" class="t-accent t-sm">skip</text>

transformer-architecture.caption: The Transformer is built from two towers: an **Encoder** (left) that reads the whole input at once, and a **Decoder** (right) that writes the output one token at a time. Attention layers let each word weigh every other word; the decoder also looks back at the encoder's summary through the highlighted **cross-attention** arrow, before a final $Linear + Softmax$ turns the numbers into word probabilities.
transformer-architecture.svg:

bert-vs-gpt.caption: **BERT** reads a sentence in both directions at once: arrows flow into the hidden **[MASK]** token from words on its left *and* right, so it guesses the missing word from full context. **GPT** only looks backward, with each word attending to earlier words to predict the next one.
bert-vs-gpt.svg:

pretrain-finetune.caption: First a model is **pre-trained** on a giant pile of unlabeled text so it picks up general language skills — this is done once and is costly. Then the **same** model is cheaply **fine-tuned** on a small labeled dataset (e.g. sentiment) to specialise it, and this adapting step can be repeated for many tasks.
pretrain-finetune.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 660 400" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Pre-training then fine-tuning: a model is pre-trained once on a huge unlabeled corpus to learn general language, then the same model is cheaply fine-tuned on small labeled task data to make a specialised model.">

  <text x="166" y="40" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-bold t-accent">STAGE 1 — Pre-training</text>

  <text x="178" y="158" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm t-muted">pre-train</text>

  <!-- general model (highlight) -->

  <text x="166" y="200" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">General language model</text>
  <text x="166" y="224" text-anchor="middle" class="t-accent">learns general language</text>
  <text x="166" y="246" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">grammar · facts · patterns</text>

  <!-- cost callout (side note) -->

</svg>

attention-masks.caption: Each grid shows which words an attention layer is allowed to look at: **rows are the word doing the looking (query), columns are the word being looked at (key)**. In **bidirectional** models like BERT every cell is open, so each word can use the whole sentence; in **causal** models like GPT only the lower triangle is open, so a word can see itself and earlier words but never the ones that come after it — the "mask hides the future."
attention-masks.svg:

bpe-tokenization.caption: BPE starts from single characters and keeps **merging the pairs that appear most often**, building reusable chunks like `es` then `est`. So a word like **"lowest"** becomes `low` + `est` — letting a small vocabulary still cover rare words.
bpe-tokenization.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 600 330" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="The word 'lowest' splits into subword tokens 'low' and 'est'; BPE builds these tokens by repeatedly merging the most frequent character pairs.">
  <text x="300" y="26" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg">Subword (BPE) Tokenization</text>
  <line x1="286" y1="46" x2="286" y2="300" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.5"/>

  <!-- LEFT: word -> subwords -->
  <text x="150" y="64" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">Input word</text>
  <rect x="95" y="74" width="110" height="38" rx="8" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="150" y="99" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold t-lg">lowest</text>

  <line x1="150" y1="114" x2="150" y2="148" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <text x="160" y="135" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm t-muted">tokenize</text>

  <rect x="76" y="156" width="64" height="38" rx="8" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="108" y="181" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold t-accent">low</text>
  <text x="150" y="181" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">+</text>
  <rect x="160" y="156" width="64" height="38" rx="8" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="192" y="181" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold t-accent">est</text>

  <text x="150" y="228" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">Rare words break into</text>
  <text x="150" y="246" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">reusable subword pieces.</text>
  <text x="150" y="278" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent t-bold">small vocab, full coverage</text>

  <!-- RIGHT: merge ladder -->
  <text x="300" y="60" text-anchor="start" class="t-bold">Merge frequent pairs</text>

  <!-- Row A: chars -->
  <rect x="300" y="74" width="24" height="26" rx="5" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="312" y="91" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">l</text>
  <rect x="327" y="74" width="24" height="26" rx="5" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="339" y="91" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">o</text>
  <rect x="354" y="74" width="24" height="26" rx="5" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="366" y="91" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">w</text>
  <rect x="381" y="74" width="24" height="26" rx="5" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="393" y="91" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">e</text>
  <rect x="408" y="74" width="24" height="26" rx="5" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="420" y="91" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">s</text>
  <rect x="435" y="74" width="24" height="26" rx="5" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="447" y="91" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">t</text>

  <line x1="380" y1="102" x2="380" y2="128" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <text x="398" y="119" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm t-accent">merge e+s → es</text>

  <!-- Row B -->
  <rect x="300" y="132" width="24" height="26" rx="5" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="312" y="149" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">l</text>
  <rect x="327" y="132" width="24" height="26" rx="5" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="339" y="149" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">o</text>
  <rect x="354" y="132" width="24" height="26" rx="5" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="366" y="149" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">w</text>
  <rect x="381" y="132" width="36" height="26" rx="5" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="399" y="149" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent t-bold">es</text>
  <rect x="420" y="132" width="24" height="26" rx="5" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="432" y="149" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">t</text>

  <line x1="380" y1="160" x2="380" y2="190" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <text x="398" y="178" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm t-accent">merge es+t → est</text>

  <!-- Row C -->
  <rect x="300" y="194" width="24" height="26" rx="5" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="312" y="211" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">l</text>
  <rect x="327" y="194" width="24" height="26" rx="5" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="339" y="211" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">o</text>
  <rect x="354" y="194" width="24" height="26" rx="5" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="366" y="211" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm">w</text>
  <rect x="381" y="194" width="40" height="26" rx="5" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="401" y="211" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent t-bold">est</text>

  <line x1="380" y1="222" x2="380" y2="252" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <text x="398" y="240" text-anchor="start" class="t-sm t-accent">merge l+o+w → low</text>

  <!-- Row D: final tokens -->
  <rect x="300" y="256" width="42" height="26" rx="6" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="321" y="273" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent t-bold">low</text>
  <rect x="348" y="256" width="42" height="26" rx="6" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="369" y="273" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent t-bold">est</text>
</svg>

lora.caption: **LoRA** keeps the huge pretrained weight matrix $W$ **frozen** and learns only two slim matrices, $B$ and $A$, whose product is added on top: the model behaves as $W + B\cdot A$. Because the rank $r$ is tiny, $B$ and $A$ hold very few numbers, so fine-tuning trains a small fraction of the parameters.
lora.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 600 350" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="LoRA low-rank adaptation: a frozen weight matrix W plus the product of two small trainable matrices B and A equals the effective weight">
  <text x="300" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-bold">LoRA: low-rank adaptation</text>

  <!-- W frozen -->
  <rect x="54" y="80" width="120" height="120" rx="6" class="f-muted s-muted" stroke-width="2"/>
  <path d="M107 140 v-5 a7 7 0 0 1 14 0 v5" class="s-line" fill="none" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="104" y="140" width="20" height="15" rx="2" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <circle cx="114" cy="146" r="2" class="s-line" fill="none" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <line x1="114" y1="147" x2="114" y2="151" class="s-line" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="114" y="222" text-anchor="middle" class="t-muted t-sm">W (frozen)</text>
  <path d="M48 80 h-6 v120 h6" class="s-muted" fill="none" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="34" y="144" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">d</text>

  <!-- plus -->
  <text x="193" y="149" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-muted">+</text>

  <!-- B tall thin -->
  <rect x="212" y="80" width="26" height="120" rx="3" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="225" y="146" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-bold t-accent">B</text>
  <text x="225" y="222" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">d × r</text>
  <path d="M212 74 v-6 h26 v6" class="s-accent" fill="none" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="225" y="62" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent">r</text>

  <!-- multiply -->
  <text x="251" y="149" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-muted">·</text>

  <!-- A wide thin -->
  <rect x="264" y="126" width="120" height="28" rx="3" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="324" y="145" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-bold t-accent">A</text>
  <text x="324" y="176" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">r × d</text>
  <path d="M264 120 h120" class="s-accent" fill="none" stroke-width="1.5" opacity="0"/>

  <!-- equals -->
  <text x="403" y="149" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg t-muted">=</text>

  <!-- result -->
  <rect x="422" y="80" width="120" height="120" rx="6" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="482" y="146" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">W + B·A</text>
  <text x="482" y="222" text-anchor="middle" class="t-muted t-sm">effective weight</text>

  <!-- legend -->
  <rect x="40" y="248" width="520" height="62" rx="8" class="f-panel2 s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="58" y="274" class="t-sm">Freeze the large matrix W. Add a low-rank update B·A   (B is d × r,  A is r × d).</text>
  <text x="58" y="296" class="t-sm t-accent">Only A and B are trained (few parameters); the rank r is much smaller than d.</text>
</svg>

neural-style-transfer.caption: **Neural style transfer** feeds a content photo and a style image through a fixed (untrained) image network, then repeatedly tweaks a new picture until it keeps the **layout** of the content image while wearing the **colors and textures** of the style image.
neural-style-transfer.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 680 400" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Neural style transfer: a content image and a style image pass through a frozen VGG CNN; content loss and style loss are minimised to make an iteratively optimised generated image.">
  <text x="340" y="26" text-anchor="middle" class="t-lg">Neural style transfer</text>

  <!-- Content image tile -->
  <text x="90" y="48" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">Content image</text>
  <rect x="30" y="54" width="120" height="96" rx="6" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <circle cx="124" cy="80" r="10" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <polygon points="40,144 72,98 104,144" class="f-muted s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <polygon points="88,144 116,110 146,144" class="f-panel2 s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="34" y1="144" x2="146" y2="144" class="s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>

  <!-- Style image tile -->
  <text x="90" y="258" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">Style image</text>
  <rect x="30" y="264" width="120" height="96" rx="6" class="f-panel s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <path d="M 72,306 C 72,292 108,292 108,314 C 108,332 78,334 78,316 C 78,306 94,306 94,316" fill="none" class="s-accent2" stroke-width="2"/>
  <path d="M 40,348 q 11,-11 22,0 t 22,0 t 22,0 t 22,0" fill="none" class="s-accent2" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <circle cx="126" cy="290" r="5" class="f-accent2 s-accent2" stroke-width="1"/>
  <circle cx="48" cy="286" r="4" class="f-accent-soft s-accent2" stroke-width="1.2"/>

  <!-- Frozen CNN (VGG) -->
  <text x="305" y="126" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">Frozen CNN (VGG)</text>
  <text x="305" y="140" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">weights fixed</text>
  <polygon points="250,150 360,178 360,252 250,290" class="f-panel2 s-line" stroke-width="2"/>
  <line x1="278" y1="157" x2="278" y2="280" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <line x1="306" y1="164" x2="306" y2="271" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <line x1="334" y1="171" x2="334" y2="261" class="s-muted" stroke-width="1.2"/>

  <!-- Inputs into CNN -->
  <path d="M 152,102 C 200,108 210,170 246,184" fill="none" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <path d="M 152,308 C 200,300 210,262 246,256" fill="none" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <!-- Loss arrows to generated image -->

  <text x="450" y="186" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">match high-level layout</text>
  <path d="M 362,193 C 440,188 470,196 536,200" fill="none" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>

  <path d="M 362,245 C 440,250 470,242 536,236" fill="none" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/>
  <text x="450" y="262" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold t-accent2">style loss</text>
  <text x="450" y="276" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">textures via Gram matrix</text>

  <!-- Generated image tile -->
  <text x="600" y="160" text-anchor="middle" class="t-bold">Generated image</text>
  <rect x="540" y="166" width="120" height="100" rx="6" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="2"/>
  <circle cx="558" cy="188" r="8" class="f-accent-soft s-accent" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <polygon points="550,256 582,214 614,256" class="f-muted s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <polygon points="602,256 630,228 656,256" class="f-panel2 s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="546" y1="256" x2="654" y2="256" class="s-line" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <path d="M 620,184 q 8,-7 16,0 t 16,0" fill="none" class="s-accent2" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <path d="M 568,238 c 0,-7 12,-7 12,1 c 0,7 -9,7 -9,1" fill="none" class="s-accent2" stroke-width="1.5"/>

  <!-- Iterative optimisation loop -->

</svg>
